Marlin Klein.lateround_profile: It’s probably safe to say that Marlin Klein loves the game of American football. Born in Germany, Klein played football for the Cologne Crocodiles before moving to the United States in high school to play in Georgia. Klein, though, never reached 25 catches or 250 yards in any of his four seasons at Michigan, and he scored just once. Of course, his time overlapped with Colston Loveland’s in Ann Arbor, and without Loveland in the mix, Klein’s role did grow in 2025 to career- bests across the board (24 catches, 248 yards, and 1 touchdown). But you still can’t really overcorrect the production enough to make him look like a standout in those departments. Beyond that, Klein rates out with bottom-five run-after- the-catch numbers among this year’s Combine-invited tight end class. The profile, overall, indicates that Klein’s most likely path to NFL snaps is to be a blocking tight end with room to grow as a receiver. It’s not the most exciting combination when seeking pure fantasy football upside, yet the 4.61-second 40-yard dash at his size gives him a 112.7 height-adjusted Speed Score. With good blocking grades and tape and good athleticism, it’s the right combination of underlying data that can still generate some optimism despite weak production data. He’s still relatively new to the game, and while that gets him some leeway, it also suggests he’s still a bit of a developmental project, one not fully NFL-ready. Nate Boerkircher.lateround_profile: Based on the receiving production, you wouldn’t expect much buzz around Nate Boerkircher in the draft process. But he’s getting some mid-round attention. Boerkircher spent his first five seasons at Nebraska (including the COVID season in 2020) before transferring to Texas A&M in (technically) his sixth season in college. It took until that 2025 season to catch more than six passes in a year, though, and even in 2025, he caught 19 balls for 198 yards. Despite that, he’s pretty consistently regarded as a tight end who will get drafted, even with that type of background. If he’s faster than projected (he didn’t run the 40), and a team really banks on the intangibles and blocking, there could be something. It would just be an all-time outlier situation with a production profile as minimal as his if he really emerged as an elite fantasy football tight end. Jaren Kanak.lateround_profile: Jaren Kanak has a really noteworthy profile, but it’s not leading to much in the way of projected draft capital. Kanak, a high school track athlete and dual-threat quarterback, was a four-star recruit as an athlete with interest from a lot of big programs. He ran a 4.52-second 40-yard dash at the Combine, equating to a height-adjusted Speed Score of 109.2. While that’s not bad by any means, virtually every tight end who ran this year graded out well by that metric. Still, the athleticism alone is noteworthy. However, he measured in at the NFL Combine at 6’2”, 234 pounds, making him both the shortest and lightest of all 27 invited tight ends. Noteworthy, though. But get this: Kanak didn’t have a catch at Oklahoma until his final season…because he was a linebacker before converting to tight end ahead of the 2025 season. Noteworthy. So, if he got a Combine invite, he must have been relevant this year, right? Yep. He went out and caught 44 passes for 533 yards, both third-best on the Sooners, while playing alongside Combine-invited wide receiver Deion Burks. Because he wound up being significantly productive, his age- and schedule-adjusted max yards per team attempt and Breakout Score are actually above average since he’s still relatively young. Transparently, Kanak has an uphill battle to fantasy football production given the size and consensus draft projections, but we’ve seen plenty of fantasy-relevant tight ends enter the league with minimal reps at the position — guys who came over from basketball, for example. It’s the type of profile that — if NFL teams considered him a Round 4 guy — could easily be putting him in the mix. Without that buy-in, we can’t get out over our skis on the speed and potential. RJ Maryland.lateround_profile: Based on age- and schedule-adjusted data, RJ Maryland is one of the better receiving tight ends in this year’s class — and, frankly, in the entire tight end database. But you wouldn’t know it based on the pre-draft hype, which is why the ZAP Score and the comps are uninspiring. Maryland, the son of Russell Maryland (the first overall pick in the 1991 NFL Draft), is projected to go undrafted most places you look. Maryland tore his ACL during the 2024 season, ending with just seven games played. But he bounced back with a solid senior year and ran a 4.51-second 40-yard dash, tied for second- fastest among runners in this year’s class. We have to point out that Maryland weighed in as the second-lightest tight end at the Combine at just 236 pounds. His weight-adjusted vertical was also poor, yet we’ve seen plenty of other pass-catching tight ends play well despite that being the case (Zach Ertz, OJ Howard, Dalton Schultz types) though those players were all bigger and heavier. Let’s look at some of that production I’ve mentioned. Maryland spent all four college seasons at Southern Methodist, churning out 113 catches, 1,495 yards, and 19 touchdowns in 46 games, finishing with a career 13.2 yards per catch rate. He also maintained at least 1.60 yards per route run, per PFF, for each of the last three seasons: really solid for a tight end. And for additional context here, Maryland put up these good numbers sharing time with Jordan Hudson (a Combine-invited receiver), Romello Brinson (a draft-eligible receiver), and Matthew Hibner (a Combine-invited tight end). Maryland was still pretty productive as a freshman with Rashee Rice logging 96 catches for 1,355 yards in 2022. But if none of this age-adjusted data and a still-young draft age (he won’t turn 22 until July) is getting NFL teams excited, then there’s not much we can do. Maryland is small, even compared to a light tight end class. He may not transition to a plus blocker in the NFL, which he wasn’t able to do in college. That might keep NFL teams from investing in his skillset. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 112244

Roman Hemby.lateround_profile: The advanced numbers don’t paint Roman Hemby as a dynamic runner. His career avoided tackles per rush rate is tied for the third-worst in this class. He gained 15 or more yards at the second-lowest rate. His yards after contact per attempt also sits in the bottom half among Combine invites. Those are rushing indicators. And they’re not flattering. The ZAP Model is a bit more forgiving because it leans heavily on receiving inputs. At Maryland, Hemby posted an 11% reception share or better in three of his four seasons — the three in which he played more than two games. His adjusted total yards per team play and adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt were below average, but not alarmingly so. Hemby then transferred to Indiana for his final season, where he set a career high with 230 carries. Once you account for offensive environment, though, his attempt share wasn’t meaningfully different from what it had been at Maryland. He never exceeded a 40% rush attempt share in college. Put it all together and the profile becomes fairly clear. Hemby projects as a committee back, likely with some pass-catching utility. That’s exactly what his statistical comparables suggest: a complementary piece who can contribute, but probably not anchor a backfield on his own.

Tory Horton.lateround_profile: Tory Horton put together a 4-catch, 48-yard, 2-touchdown performance in Week 9 against Washington. And then we didn’t see him on a football field again. Groin and shin injuries cut Horton’s rookie season short, and that absence likely played a role in Seattle trading for Rashid Shaheed at the deadline. Before the injury, Horton was doing his best Isaac TeSlaa impression, scoring five touchdowns on just 22 targets. As we’ve already established, those touchdown totals aren’t particularly predictive. And unfortunately, the underlying metrics for Horton don’t offer much reassurance, either. Horton finished Year 1 with modest efficiency marks, posting 1.16 yards per route run and a 16% targets-per-route rate. His 10.6% target share wasn’t notable, either. Still, there’s a reasonable case to give Horton the benefit of the doubt. We’re working entirely with early-season data, and we know rookie wide receivers often take a step forward during the second half of their first campaigns. Horton also graded out reasonably well as a prospect — his 31.5 ZAP score as the 166th overall pick resulted in a strong Looking ahead, there’s at least some reason to like Horton as a talent, but the Seahawks re-signing Shaheed definitely hurts his short-term outlook. We shouldn’t view Horton as anything more than a deep bench stash at this point.

Malik Benson.lateround_profile: Malik Benson can absolutely run. The former track athlete clocked a 4.37 40 at the Combine, and when you watch him, the vertical juice is obvious. He’s built to threaten defenses over the top. The path to this point, though, has been anything but linear. Benson spent two seasons in JUCO before landing at Alabama in 2023, where he caught just 13 passes across 14 games. That led to a transfer to Florida State, where he managed only 25 receptions. He then finished his career at Oregon in 2025, his fourth college stop. That final season was technically his most productive, but even then, his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt peaked at just 1.88. That’s a bottom-10 mark in this class. Deployment helps explain some of it. Over his final three non-JUCO seasons, Benson posted a 25.6% deep-ball target rate, 11th-highest in the class. He was used as a field- stretcher. The problem is that he was rarely featured. He never reached even a 15% prorated target share in any season, the third-worst rate among Combine invite wide receivers. So what we’re looking at is a 23-and-a-half-year-old prospect with plus speed, minimal target-earning ability, and no season that truly stands out from a production standpoint. The athleticism gives him a puncher’s chance. But analytically, this is a thin profile. It’s tough to get overly optimistic without meaningful draft capital to force opportunity.

Omar Cooper.lateround_profile: Looking at Omar Cooper’s statistical comps gives you a good sense of the archetype. He’s a create-after-the-catch receiver who can make defenders miss. Among this year’s Combine invitees, Cooper ranks third-best in career missed tackles forced per reception. Notably, the two players ahead of him failed to record a Breakout Score. Cooper’s Breakout Score is solid, but not special at 41.7 — roughly the 54th percentile. That middling mark is largely due to the fact that he didn’t post a truly strong adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt season until 2025, his fourth and final year at Indiana. Non-receiving work is helping Cooper’s ZAP score, though. He shared the field with fellow Combine invite Elijah Sarratt, and he contributed on the ground as well, totaling 97 rushing yards and a pair of scores over his final two seasons. It’s a well-rounded profile, even if it lacks a truly dominant season. The Deebo Samuel comparison is particularly interesting. Samuel owns a thicker build, but analytically, they’re closer than you might think. Their Breakout Scores are just five points apart. Their career yards per route run marks were 2.47 (Cooper) and 2.43 (Samuel). Their first downs per route run rates were nearly identical. And their best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt seasons were 2.63 and 2.64. The key difference was deployment. Samuel saw a lot more manufactured looks behind the line of scrimmage, and he was rarely a slot receiver at South Carolina, never topping a 20% slot rate in any season, per PFF. Cooper, meanwhile, finished his career with a 47.5% slot rate. That’s not disqualifying or anything. Cooper’s perimeter yards per route run was 2.40, actually 0.19 higher than Samuel’s collegiate mark. That outside efficiency ranks ninth- best in this class and fifth-best among wide receivers projected to go inside the top 100. There’s a lot to like here. Cooper brings the versatility the ZAP Model values, and even though he wasn’t deployed as a full-time boundary receiver, he showed he could handle it when asked. That gives us dynasty managers something to work with.

Chimere Dike.lateround_profile: Chimere Dike was a pleasant surprise from the 2025 wide receiver class. His peripherals weren’t eye-popping — he averaged just 1.02 yards per route run — but relative to expectations, Dike clearly outperformed them. After all, he finished the season leading the NFL in all-purpose yards. That return-game production doesn’t move the needle much for fantasy football, but it does speak to Dike’s athleticism and ability with the ball in his hands — traits that should help him stick around the league. The biggest problem for Dike right now is the Titans’ signing of Wan’Dale Robinson. Brian Daboll brought in his former weapon from New York, and Robinson has been a career slot player, even if that slot usage dipped a bit in 2025. Dike lived in the slot as a rookie, running 68% of his routes from that area of the field. The Titans also gave him a shallower role — 33% of his targets came behind the line of scrimmage, per PFF data. That usage likely signals some limitations in his overall route profile, and those cheaper looks could now find their way to Robinson. Not ideal. Pure slot receivers are becoming less fantasy relevant as offenses lean more into heavier personnel groupings, and Dike didn’t enter the league as a standout route runner. Now he has Wan’Dale Robinson to deal with. You should keep him on your roster, but the odds of him becoming a significant fantasy contributor took a hit during free agency.

Quinshon Judkins.lateround_profile: Quinshon Judkins’ 2025 season was a win for fantasy football. Back in August, we weren’t even sure he’d suit up for the Browns. Instead, he delivered an impressive — albeit potentially slightly overrated — rookie campaign. “Overrated” might sound harsh, so let me provide some context. Judkins came out firing, with his three best rushing yards over expected per attempt games occurring within his first four contests. Fantasy managers were understandably excited. From there, the efficiency tapered off. That doesn’t mean he was bad. Far from it. Judkins averaged over 12 PPR points per game — a mark only four other running backs in the class reached. And he did so in brutal conditions. Among higher-volume backs, he ranked dead last in yards before contact per attempt, a clear reflection of Cleveland’s offensive line issues. Pro Football Focus graded the Browns’ run blocking as bottom-five in the league. Where there is reason for mild concern is in the passing game. Judkins’ ZAP score wasn’t super elite coming out because he was never deployed as a full- time receiving back in college. That theme carried over to the NFL. As a rookie, he ran a route on just 30% of Cleveland’s dropbacks, a lower participation rate than Derrick Henry. That kind of usage can cap a running back’s fantasy ceiling. It’s not necessarily a talent deficiency — Judkins isn’t incapable as a receiver — but Cleveland preferred to use fellow rookie Dylan Sampson in more obvious passing situations. A new coaching staff could shift that deployment, but Sampson is a capable player who figures to remain part of the equation throughout their rookie contracts. Judkins is a good player. Arguably the best pure runner in the 2025 class. But Cleveland’s offensive outlook is shaky, and without growth in receiving usage, his path to true RB1 upside narrows. That’s not a massive red flag — it’s just something to keep in mind.

Chase Roberts.lateround_profile: The last time a BYU wide receiver was drafted was 2023. You might’ve heard of him — Puka Nacua. Those are pretty large shoes to fill for Chase Roberts. Let’s be clear, though: Roberts is almost certainly not the next Nacua. For starters, he’ll be 25 at draft time. In ZAP Model history, only four wide receivers have entered the league at 25 or older. The most notable recent example is Devaughn Vele — and even that’s more “solid depth piece” than fantasy cornerstone. Like Vele, Roberts is older largely because of a mission trip out of high school, which delayed his collegiate timeline. That context is important to know, but age still feeds directly into the model. Because of it, Roberts didn’t register a Breakout Score. If we pretend he entered college immediately and was 23 instead of 25, his Breakout Score would’ve been 30.9, which isn’t amazing anyway. At BYU, Roberts improved each season, peaking at a 2.39 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark in 2025. He was a reliable presence in the offense, finishing with the sixth-highest first downs per route run rate in this class. Roberts has solid size and played both inside and outside in college. Most impressively, his perimeter yards per route run was 2.61 — fifth-best in the class. That’s not nothing. The age is what drags this profile down more than anything else. Without it, Roberts would still be more “interesting Day 3 flier” than priority target, but there’d be less friction.

Kaden Wetjen.lateround_profile: Chances are, whoever drafts Kaden Wetjen is doing so with special teams in mind. He returned 56 kicks and 54 punts during his three seasons at Iowa — after beginning his career at Iowa Western Community College — and his return work earned him the 2024 Jet Award as the nation’s top return specialist. That’s the selling point. From a receiving standpoint, Wetjen is one of the least productive wide receivers in ZAP Model history. He does get a bump for versatility — late-round prospects with any sort of differentiating trait tend to look slightly better in the model than pure replacement- level profiles. But the résumé through the air is extremely thin. Wetjen caught just 23 passes across three seasons at Iowa, and over 54% of his targets came behind the line of scrimmage. That aligns with how he was used: get him the ball in space and let him create. Unsurprisingly, he left college without a Breakout Score. Wetjen actually owns the second-lowest best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark in the ZAP Model database. His best-season adjusted fantasy points per game also ranks in the bottom two. And that’s out of hundreds of wide receivers. That’s…not encouraging. To his credit, he reportedly impressed at the Shrine Bowl, and Iowa’s offense hasn’t exactly been known for high-volume passing attacks. But historically, when collegiate receiving production is this limited, the NFL fantasy translation rate is near zero. Wetjen has a unique profile. It’s just one that’s far more likely to matter in the return game than in your rookie drafts.

Jadarian Price.lateround_profile: Jadarian Price is probably the most difficult analytical evaluation in this class. That’s what happens when you spend your career behind a likely first-round back. Price ruptured his Achilles as a freshman and didn’t record a touch. His second season was quiet, as you’d expect. In 2024 and 2025, he handled just 120 and 113 carries while Jeremiyah Love soaked up the majority of the workload. Across his entire college career, Price caught only 15 passes — a glaring red flag for fantasy translation. Price’s best-season prorated reception share was 2.8%. In ZAP Model history, 18 backs have fallen below the 3% threshold. None became fantasy-relevant. If you expand to backs below 5%, there are 46 of them — and only Derrick Henry produced a 10-plus PPR points-per-game season within his first three years. Draft capital could change the equation. If Price goes inside the top 100, he’d join only Henry, D’Onta Foreman, and Ty Davis-Price as backs with that level of investment and a sub-5% reception share. The problem? The two good to elite backs in that sample, Henry and Foreman, were dominant collegiate runners with strong adjusted total yards per team play marks. Price’s rate sits at 1.12 — well below that tier — in large part because he was a secondary option in his offense. The model does attempt to account for that via Teammate Score, but that adjustment is inherently imprecise. It’s much easier to evaluate a back who owned most of his backfield than one who shared it with a star. Price’s score gets a boost, but the range of outcomes around that boost is wide. There is a path. If the draft capital hits, Price becomes an outlier bet. And that’s where subjectivity can reasonably enter the process. But the athletic profile complicates things. Price entered the Combine with the second- best career 15-plus-yard run rate among invites, suggesting real big-play juice. But at 203 pounds with a 4.49 40-yard dash, his Speed Score came in at 99.9 — solid, not special. And here’s the historical hurdle: no back in the ZAP Model database has reached 10 PPR points per game in the NFL after posting a sub-105 Speed Score, an adjusted total yards per team play below 1.20 (Price: 1.12), and an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt below 0.40 (Price: 0.30). There are contextual reasons for those numbers. But at some point, context stops being an explanation and starts being an excuse.

Devin Neal.lateround_profile: Devin Neal more or less did what the ZAP Model suggested he would do as a rookie. Was he great? Probably not. Was he playable? Absolutely. Thanks to injuries to Alvin Kamara and Kendre Miller, Neal found himself operating as New Orleans’ lead back for stretches of the season. He logged a 70% snap share three times, and in those games, he averaged a respectable 12.1 PPR points per contest. Neal entered the league projecting as a potential three-down back — just not a dominant one. His collegiate profile suggested competence across the board without a true standout trait. And that’s exactly what we saw in 2025. When given the opportunity, he handled early downs, ran routes at a high clip, and stayed on the field in passing situations. In those three high-usage games, his route participation exceeded 70%, leading to an 11% target share. In other words, the coaching staff trusted him. It tells us that if injuries strike again, Neal is capable of absorbing a near three-down workload. It may not be explosive or hyper-efficient, but it’s usable volume. Efficiency-wise, though, nothing jumped off the page. His success rate was low and similar to Kendre Miller’s. His 10-plus-yard run rate lagged behind most of his rookie classmates. And his receiving efficiency was below the norm. He didn’t separate. But he also didn’t crater. And that’s really the story here. Neal profiles as a stabilizer — the type of back who can keep an offense on schedule and deliver replacement-level fantasy production when called upon. He’s unlikely to become a league-winner, but he’s also unlikely to disappear. In dynasty, that archetype has value. He’s a high-end depth piece with contingent upside — the kind of player you’re glad to roster when the inevitable running back injuries hit. Not extraordinary. But very rosterable. Closing Football isn’t solved. It never will be. If it were, there’d be no reason to do this. No reason to build models. No reason to debate landing spots or argue over breakout ages. No reason to care about a 21-year-old running back’s target share. But this game lives in the gray area. Humans are playing it. Coaches are making decisions. Front offices are guessing. Injuries happen. Situations change. And we’re all trying to make sense of it — to reverse engineer a sport built on chaos. That’s what makes prospecting so addicting. There’s always room for improvement. This guide isn’t about certainty. It’s about probability. It’s about stacking small edges over time. It’s about having a process you trust when you’re on the clock and the group chat is losing its mind. We’ll get things right. We’ll get things wrong. That’s part of it. But we’ll keep refining. Keep questioning. Keep iterating. Because the edge isn’t in being perfect — it’s in being thoughtful. Thanks for letting me obsess over this stuff for another year. Makai Lemon.lateround_profile: If you want a quick-and-dirty comp for Makai Lemon, think Amon-Ra St. Brown, another USC receiver. St. Brown is a popular stylistic comparison — both are technicians with elite short-area quickness and a relentless edge to their game. Like St. Brown, Lemon has that dog in him. Interestingly, St. Brown doesn’t surface as a direct comp in the ZAP Model, largely because Lemon’s analytical profile is actually stronger. And that’s saying something — St. Brown entered the league with a great résumé and a low-risk Draft Capital Delta tag. Lemon played three seasons at USC and improved every year. He caught just six passes as a freshman, but that jumped to 52 as a sophomore and 77 as a junior. His prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt hit 2.98 last season and climbed to 3.35 when adjusted for strength of schedule. That was the third-best top-season adjusted mark in the class. That production translated to a Breakout Score of 67.1, also third-best among Combine invitees. Lemon’s deployment is where things get interesting. The Trojans had Lemon run more than 75% of his routes from the slot in college. St. Brown was at 69%, per PFF. That deployment isn’t irrelevant. Over the last five years, Day 2 receivers with slot rates north of 70% have generally struggled. The first-rounders in that bucket, however — Jaylen Waddle, Treylon Burks, Kadarius Toney, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, and Emeka Egbuka — have had much better outcomes. And stylistically and statistically, Lemon looks nothing like Burks or Toney. There’s naturally a bit more volatility in a profile with heavy slot usage. But as was the case with Jaxon Smith-Njigba when he entered the league, draft capital tells the story. If a team invests in Lemon in Round 1 — possibly early Round 1 — they’re likely viewing him as more than just a slot specialist. And that projection isn’t blind optimism. Lemon actually posted the best career perimeter yards per route run rate among this year’s NFL Combine invites. In other words, he can win outside. Lemon is the real deal. Even if Nelson Agholor shows up as a top statistical comp.

Desmond Reid.lateround_profile: Desmond Reid out of Pittsburgh (Hail 2 Pitt) isn’t just the smallest back in this year’s class — at 174 pounds, he’s the lightest running back in the ZAP Model database since 2017. That size is going to be the headline. And it should be. But if you zoom in on the production, it’s legitimately strong. Reid posted the second- best top-season reception share in the class at 20.4%. His adjusted receiving yards per team play also ranked second. And his best-season adjusted total yards per team play — which includes rushing — came in at 2.10, a mark only two running backs in the class topped. From a pure production standpoint, Reid is near the top of this group. The problem is the body type. In the ZAP Model database, there are 23 running backs under 190 pounds. Only De’Von Achane — who added weight once he reached the NFL — and Tarik Cohen have delivered truly fantasy-relevant seasons. You could maybe toss Keaton Mitchell into the conversation, but that’s not exactly a deep bench of hits. So the bet with Reid is pretty clear: you’re hoping for a creative offensive coordinator who leans into his receiving ability and builds a role around him. In the right system, he could be a PPR weapon. The challenge is that the NFL has shifted. Running back target spikes aren’t quite what they were in the Tarik Cohen era. It’s harder now for a sub-180-pound back to live purely off receiving volume without a lot of rushing work. Pitt allegiance aside — that’s my alma mater, for those of you who don’t know — Reid is fun. He’s quick, he’s productive as a receiver, and he’s electric in space. But fun doesn’t automatically translate to fantasy difference-maker — especially at 174 pounds. Le’Veon Moss • RB The issue with Le’Veon Moss isn’t the name — it absolutely sounds like an NFL running back’s name. It’s the production. Moss spent four years at Texas A&M and never once handled more than 25% of his team’s rush attempts in a season. His best-season attempt share was only higher than Eli Heidenreich’s in this class, and Heidenreich is basically a slot receiver playing running back. The receiving profile doesn’t bail Moss out. He posted just a 6.7% best-season reception share, the third-lowest mark among Combine invite running backs. His adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt came in at 0.63, well below the class average. So we’ve got a limited rushing share and limited receiving involvement. At the Combine, Moss weighed 203 pounds. That’s not tiny, but it’s also not the kind of frame that screams “early-down hammer” — especially when the production doesn’t support that label. Historically, that combination is rough. In the ZAP Model database, only four running backs have reached 10 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons with both a sub-8% best-season reception share and a sub-30% best-season attempt share: Chris Carson, Dameon Pierce, Damien Harris, and CJ Anderson. Pierce and Harris had decent draft capital. All four of them were over 215 pounds entering the league. Moss doesn’t check either of those boxes. Which brings us to the key question: where does Moss fit? He doesn’t project as a clear passing-down option. He doesn’t profile as a high-volume early-down grinder. He didn’t dominate touches in college. And as a likely Day 3 pick, he won’t have investment to force opportunity. That’s an uncomfortable place to live for fantasy purposes. Without a standout trait or a defined role to lean on, Moss feels more like depth than a back you’ll be excited to roster.

Emeka Egbuka.lateround_profile: Emeka Egbuka’s rookie season is difficult to summarize without sounding dramatic. Because the production split is genuinely extreme. Over the first five weeks of the season, Egbuka averaged more than 20 PPR points per game. From that point forward, his average fell to just 8.3. That’s not a gradual decline – that’s a cliff. When researching his profile, the first thing I wanted to know was whether this kind of early-season surge followed by a prolonged drop-off actually shows up in historical data. Have we seen rookie wide receivers start this strong and then fade this dramatically? The answer? Not really. Among Year 2 Model wide receivers, a drop like this simply hasn’t happened. Puka Nacua averaged over 20 PPR points per game through Week 6 as a rookie, but finished the season closer to 16. Calvin Ridley is a better comparison – he averaged 19.7 PPR points per game through Week 4 of his rookie year before falling to 10.7 the rest of the way. Even then, that decline wasn’t nearly as severe as what we saw from Egbuka. That’s why it’s important to zoom out and look at the full sample. Egbuka entered the league with a strong ZAP score, and his full-season rookie numbers were impressive: a 1.75 yards per route run rate, a target share just shy of 24%, and the second-most first downs among wideouts in his draft class. Egbuka also lands in excellent company with his Year 2 Model score of 77.7. Within the Year 2 Model database – prior to this most recent draft class – we’ve seen eight wide receivers with Year 2 scores within three points of Egbuka’s who also posted a yards-per-route-run rate north of 1.60. Those players? Allen Robinson, DJ Moore, DK Metcalf, Terry McLaurin, Chase Claypool, Amon-Ra St. Brown, Rashee Rice, and Zay Flowers. Not bad. We shouldn’t call Egbuka bust-proof – just take a look at that first Journey Comparable — but landing in the Elite Producer category gives him strong odds to be at least a WR2 in fantasy football moving forward. Especially now that Mike Evans is gone. And especially when his 58% catchable targets rate – a 4th percentile number – regresses favorably.

Antonio Williams.lateround_profile: Antonio Williams has kind of a weird production profile. As a freshman at Clemson, he caught 56 passes for 604 yards and 4 touchdowns. On the surface, that looks strong — especially since he led the team in receptions and yards. But Clemson threw the ball 480 times that season, which muted the impact. His receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 1.26, so the Breakout Score didn’t get much of a boost. Injuries limited him to five games as a sophomore. Then, over his final two seasons — again at Clemson — his receiving yards per team pass attempt never exceeded 1.70. In other words, Williams was consistently productive. He just never delivered a high-end season. That resulted in a 38.4 Breakout Score (48th percentile). Historically, that’s a tough needle to thread. Across the ZAP Model database, only seven wide receivers have posted a 12-plus PPR points-per-game season within their first three NFL campaigns after failing to record a max-season receiving yards per team pass attempt above 2.0. Williams topped out at 1.70. Lower the threshold to 1.80, and the list shrinks to Tyreek Hill, Hunter Renfrow, Terry McLaurin, and Ladd McConkey. Hill won with generational traits. Renfrow’s peak was short- lived. McConkey, ironically his top comp, had elite per-route efficiency that wasn’t fully reflected in his raw team-share numbers. McLaurin is simply an outlier. That context suggests Williams has an uphill analytical battle. There are positives. He offers versatility — 25 carries for 187 rushing yards during his Clemson career and more than 40 punt returns. That multifaceted usage hints at ways he can carve out a role early. The concern is role type. Williams ran nearly 80% of his routes from the slot in college. That type of deployment in the NFL would really limit his fantasy upside. Williams looks like the type of player who can help a team move the chains and contribute in multiple phases. We should question how that translates to fantasy football, though, unless he finds a team very willing to pepper him with looks. At least there’s a nice top comparable to keep us interested.

Kyle Monangai.lateround_profile: The ZAP Model wasn’t particularly fond of Kyle Monangai for two primary reasons: draft capital and receiving. As a seventh-round pick, he didn’t have meaningful investment behind him. And analytically, the pass-game background was thin. His adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt sat well below the 2025 class average, and he never reached an 8% best-season reception share in college. If Monangai was going to hit, it was going to be on the ground. Fortunately, he landed in a spot that allowed that to happen. In Chicago, Monangai handled 169 regular-season carries. He became just the fourth Year 2 Model back to reach at least 160 rookie rush attempts after being selected outside the top 200 picks. The other three: the aforementioned Jacory Croskey-Merritt, James Robinson, and fellow Rutgers alum Isiah Pacheco — ironically Monangai’s top Journey Comparable. So what’s next? The receiving work didn’t expand in Year 1. Monangai finished with just a 5.7% target share and an underwhelming 0.75 yards per route run rate. That’s unlikely to fuel a fantasy ceiling. As a rusher, he was solid — and undeniably fun to watch — but the underlying efficiency was more average than highlight reels suggest. PFF graded him below average in missed tackles forced per attempt, and Next Gen Stats had his rushing yards over expected per attempt hovering around league average. Where he did stand out was in consistency. Among 48 qualified backs, Monangai ranked fifth in success rate. But even that deserves context: teammate D’Andre Swift ranked just below him, and Chicago’s offensive line finished fifth in run-blocking grade, per PFF. The environment was doing some of the lifting. Monangai isn’t a bad player. In fact, relative to expectation, he’s a ZAP Model L. But the receiving ceiling remains limited, and his rushing profile looks more early-down grinder than true difference-maker. My personal rankings are higher on Monangai than what the Year 2 Model is saying, largely because the situation is fantastic. The model isn’t accounting for that. But the broader profile tells a more measured story. Long term, he projects as a flex option with occasional RB2 spikes, not unlike Isiah Pacheco. He’s probably not some foundational piece. That makes the Flex Play designation appropriate.

Malachi Fields.lateround_profile: The concern with Malachi Fields’ profile is simple: the production never truly arrived. Fields began his collegiate career at Virginia in 2021, catching just 11 passes as a true freshman before suffering a foot injury that limited him to one game as a sophomore. His junior season showed promise — 58 receptions, 811 yards, and 5 touchdowns — resulting in a solid 2.24 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. Unfortunately, that was the peak. Fields played two more seasons — one at Virginia and one at Notre Dame — but neither delivered a stronger statistical campaign. For a player with five collegiate seasons, the lack of a serious breakout is notable. And that’s the crux of the issue. Fields brings rare size at 6’5”, 218 pounds, but his Breakout Score sits at a below- average 33.6. His best season in yards per route run was 2.20, ranking 34th among the 46 wide receivers in this class. His career mark was well below 2.0. If Fields is selected on Day 2, as projected, he’ll need to buck recent historical trends. Since 2020, only nine wide receivers drafted between Picks 33 and 100 posted a best-season yards per route run below 2.30. Fields falls short of that threshold, as you just read. That group includes the following: Van Jefferson, Josh Palmer, Jonathan Mingo, Keon Coleman, Adonai Mitchell, Ja’Lynn Polk, Luke McCaffrey, Savion Williams, and Isaac TeSlaa. There are a whole lot of draft busts in there. There’s size to dream on, but the body of work suggests he’ll need significant development to outkick the profile. An older breakout and the absence of a truly elite season leave little analytical margin for error. Ja’Kobi Lane • WR Ja’Kobi Lane is one of just five early-declare wide receivers in this year’s class, spending all three collegiate seasons at USC. After catching just seven passes in six games as a freshman, he increased that total to 43 and 49 over his next two seasons. The production profile, however, isn’t overly impressive. His 2024 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark was just 1.14, rising to a respectable 2.35 as a junior. That best-season figure still ranked outside the top-20 in the class. The result was a 35.9 Breakout Score — a below-average number within the ZAP Model database. To be fair, Lane shared the field with likely first-rounder Makai Lemon throughout his career, so Teammate Score is giving him a modest boost. But even accounting for that context, the résumé is fairly ordinary. His career yards per route run, per PFF, ranks 26th in the class. He was serviceable at generating first downs, but nothing in the production profile screams difference-maker. Lane’s draft buzz likely stems from the role he could play for an NFL team. He has rare length for a prototypical X receiver, and that body type remains in high demand. There are concerns, though. While long, Lane’s frame is leaner than you’d expect for a true contested-catch specialist — and that was a major part of his game in college. Among this year’s Combine invitees, Lane ranked first with a near 33% contested target rate. If he’s selected inside the top 100, as projected, he’d become just the fourth wideout since 2020 drafted in that range with a career contested target rate above 30%. The other three? Josh Palmer, Nico Collins, and Alec Pierce. That’s not bad company. But each of those receivers entered the league with stronger Breakout Scores and more mass to support their contested-catch profiles. That likely aided in their transition into the NFL. Lane isn’t a bad prospect. Early declaration plus physical tools always provide a path. But analytically, he profiles more as a bet on traits than on demonstrated production. And those bets tend to carry more variance than upside certainty.

Zachariah Branch.lateround_profile: Zachariah Branch weighs just 177 pounds, so it’s no surprise that 77.5% of his routes came from the slot during his three-year collegiate career, the third-highest rate in this class. That deployment was consistent across stops. At USC, his slot rates were 74% and 79% during his two seasons played there. After transferring to Georgia for his final year, that number stayed at 79%. The speed and shiftiness pop immediately on film. That elusiveness is almost certainly driving his projected draft capital. But analytically, there are red flags. Branch’s production never truly got there. His raw receiving yards per team pass attempt failed to reach 2.0 in a season, and even when adjusted strength of schedule, he maxed out at 2.11. The result was a very ordinary 31.2 Breakout Score. The usage profile is even more concerning. An enormous 45.5% of Branch’s career targets came behind the line of scrimmage. That doesn’t mean he can’t grow into something more at the next level, but it does mean the projection requires a significant leap. Historically, that’s been dangerous territory. If Branch is selected inside the top 100 — as NFL Mock Draft Database currently projects — he’ll be the 11th player drafted in that range since 2020 with a career behind-the-line target rate above 30%. And the first to exceed 40%. Only two players in that bucket — Wan’Dale Robinson and Luther Burden — posted Breakout Scores above 75. The others? Lynn Bowden, Amari Rodgers, Anthony Schwartz, Kadarius Toney, Rondale Moore, Velus Jones, Tre Tucker, and Malachi Corley. Big yikes. A large driver of that is screen usage, with over 35% of Branch’s career yards came on screens. Among top-100 picks since 2020, only two wideouts saw over 30% of their career yardage total stem from screens: Malachi Corley and Anthony Schwartz. Bigger yikes. To put it bluntly, this archetype has burned teams before. If Branch receives strong draft capital and dynasty managers push him up rookie boards, it’ll be a tough sell. That’s especially true in an NFL that’s leaned into heavier personnel packages, reducing the standalone fantasy dominance of pure slot specialists. You can squint and see a Tyreek Hill-like prospect with Branch, but that’s not something we should ever bank on.

Matthew Hibner.lateround_profile: Matthew Hibner is one of two tight ends from Southern Methodist invited to the NFL Combine (the other being RJ Maryland). That alone can complicate his receiving profile a bit. Looking at the data before accounting for that competition, Hibner didn’t do much to stand out. He spent six years in college (four at Michigan and two at Southern Methodist) and failed to crack 1.0 max yards per team attempt after adjusting for age and schedule. He didn’t see much volume (two catches in three years at Michigan) until his time at Southern Methodist, when he hauled in 24 passes for 368 yards in his first year as a transfer. He built on that with a 31-catch, 436-yard line in his final season and scored four times in both of his seasons with the Mustangs. Not only did Hibner play alongside Maryland, but Romello Brinson and Jordan Hudson are also draft-eligible wide receivers, with Hudson also getting a Combine invite. It was a fairly crowded target tree. Still, we can’t ignore the lack of production at Michigan and the late emergence of Hibner. With that said, the 4.57-second 40-yard dash is good for a height-adjusted Speed Score of 115.1. It’s just unlikely to lead to much at all, given the receiving profile and undrafted projection for Hibner. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 112211

Riley Nowakowski.lateround_profile: Riley Nowakowski played six years in college, five of them at Wisconsin, with his final year at Indiana. It took a long while for him to find his footing as a tight end, and he didn’t log more than 11 catches or 74 yards until his final season, when he caught 32 balls in 16 games for 387 yards (24.2 yards per game). Adjusting all of that for age and schedule, Nowakowski just finds himself near the bottom of the tight end database in meaningful receiving metrics and Breakout Score. Despite being a short tight end (6’2”, 250), Nowakowski actually profiles more as a blocking tight end than an undersized pure receiver and spent most of his career as an inline player. He rates out as well as he does because tight end is a tricky position to examine for fantasy football purposes. His traits are getting him top-15 tight end draft capital projections as a top-200 pick. A top-200 pick who’s primarily a blocker still has a better fantasy football outlook than a great pass-catching tight end who isn’t even on a team. There’s just not a lot here to indicate future fantasy football output in a needle-moving capacity. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 112200

Miles Kitselman.lateround_profile: Poor athletic testing was rare for tight ends at the Combine this year. Of course, only 20 of 27 Combine-invited tight ends ran the 40, but of those who did, only one ran slower than a 4.74: Miles Kitselman (4.90). That equates to an 88.2 height- adjusted Speed Score. That’s 12th-percentile in the tight end model. That doesn’t automatically mean there’s nothing in the profile to get excited about. Because even with that athleticism, Kitselman is drawing late-round draft projections, which makes him more interesting than most slow tight ends. It can mean there’s something here with the production. Was there? Eh. Kitselman started off his college career at Hutchinson Community College and then spent two years at Alabama before transferring elsewhere in the SEC to Tennessee. In 2024, Kitselman was fifth in receiving (301 yards) for the Volunteers in a crowded offense with Dont’e Thornton, Bru McCoy, and Chris Brazzell. He was fifth again in receiving (253 yards) in 2025 and struggled with drops. There’s a lack of explosiveness in the data (his career-long catch is 31 yards), and his vertical and broad jumps were weak, as well. Even if drafted, Kitselman rates out more as a helpful piece to the offense as a strong blocker than as a fantasy football stud. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 112266

Woody Marks.lateround_profile: Woody Marks didn’t profile as a true three-down back entering the NFL. That’s what the ZAP Model suggested, and it’s what I believed, too. And yet, as a rookie, we frequently saw him in that role. Marks finished with the sixth- best weighted opportunity per game in the class. He also became just the 12th running back in Year 2 Model history to log at least 190 rookie carries after being selected outside the top 100 picks. Sounds impressive. Historically, it isn’t. Of the 11 backs who did it before him, their average best PPR points-per-game season across Years 2 through 4 is just 7.9. That’s not difference-making production. When Day 3 running backs earn heavy rookie volume, it’s often more about team weakness than individual talent. Opportunity can be circumstantial. That doesn’t mean Marks is a bad player. In fact, the ZAP Model viewed him fairly favorably relative to his draft capital. But even last year’s guide framed him as more of a complementary back with pass-catching ability than a true three-down option. The strange part of his rookie season? Despite the workload, Houston didn’t feature him heavily as a receiver. Marks posted just a 6.9% target share on a roughly 40% route participation rate — solid, but not what you’d expect for a player whose profile leaned on receiving utility. Efficiency didn’t help the case. Behind a below-average Houston run-blocking unit, Marks ranked 43rd out of 48 qualified backs in success rate, 33rd in rushing yards over expected per attempt, and 42nd in EPA per rush. Among the nine rookie backs with 100-plus carries, his rates of 10-plus-yard and 20-plus-yard runs both ranked second worst. That’s not a profile that screams long-term stability. It’s no surprise, then, that Houston traded for David Montgomery. Hypothetically, Montgomery’s a strong complement — he can handle much of the in-between-the-tackles work the Texans thought Nick Chubb might provide in 2025. That’s going to severely limit Marks’ workload, though you could argue Montgomery is a win for Marks. If they don’t add a back with significant capital in the draft, Montgomery isn’t overly difficult competition compared to what could’ve been for Houston. Even still, we’re back where we started: Marks is a tandem back, not a bell-cow.

Emmett Johnson.lateround_profile: When you look strictly at ZAP Model inputs, Emmett Johnson may have the strongest pure production profile of any running back in this class. The dude can catch. Johnson proved that over four seasons at Nebraska, hauling in 85 receptions across his final two years. That translated to a best-season prorated reception share of 19.2% and a peak adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt of 1.13 — both top-three marks in the class. For fantasy purposes, that’s huge. The question is what he brings as a runner. Among the 20 Combine-invited running backs this year, Johnson’s career yards after contact per attempt ranks third-worst, per PFF. His explosive run rate also sits in the bottom half of the class. On the surface, that suggests limited tackle-breaking and chunk-play upside. There’s some nuance, though. Per Rich Hribar of Sharp Football Analysis, Johnson was hit at or behind the line on a class-low 33.9% of his runs. And when he was hit at or behind the line, he gained yardage on a class-high 75.3% of those carries. That hints at some subtle efficiency that box-score metrics don’t fully capture. Johnson’s unlikely to be a big-play threat in the NFL, though — you can see that with his career explosive run rate. He also ran a 4.56 40-yard dash at 202 pounds, which led to a mediocre 93.4 Speed Score. But that doesn’t automatically disqualify him. Since 2011, 10 running backs weighing under 210 pounds have produced a 12-plus PPR points- per-game B2S after being drafted outside Round 2. Only 4 of those 10 had a Speed Score above 94.0. In other words, elite straight-line speed hasn’t exactly been a prerequisite for a back like Johnson. We’ve seen receiving-centric backs with modest athletic testing hit before — Kyren Williams, Bucky Irving, Aaron Jones. We’ve also seen it fail, of course, and it fails more often than not. But the range is wide. Realistically, Johnson likely projects as a third-down back in many offenses. But in the right ecosystem — where receiving usage turns into full-scope opportunity — there’s at least a path to something more. And in this running back class, that kind of path isn’t incredibly common.

Seth McGowan.lateround_profile: Prior to this draft class, only one ZAP Model running back — Martez Carter — entered the NFL at age 25 or older. This year, we have two. One of them is Seth McGowan. Does age matter? Yes. The model accounts for it, and historically, older prospects have produced weaker outcomes. But context is needed. College football has shifted dramatically in recent years. Transfer rules changed. The pandemic extended eligibility. NIL deals altered incentives. As a result, we’re seeing older backs more frequently. Over the last two draft classes (excluding this one), 10 running backs either attended the NFL Combine or were drafted at age 24 or older. From 2011 through 2023, there were 11 total. Last year alone, RJ Harvey, Woody Marks, and Jacory Croskey-Merritt were all 24 or older at selection. Adjusted for draft capital, the hit rates for these older prospects haven’t been disastrous of late. As long as the production profiles were strong. McGowan’s profile isn’t bad — it’s just uneven. He began at Oklahoma in 2020 before being dismissed following legal issues, though the charges were ultimately dropped. After time away from the game, he resurfaced at Butler Community College in 2023, then transferred to New Mexico State in 2024. There, he logged 152 carries in a backfield that also featured 2026 draft hopeful Mike Washington. More importantly for his analytical outlook, McGowan posted a career-best 16.3% reception share that year. That season did a lot of the heavy lifting for his pass-catching profile, showing at least some ability to function on passing downs. The question is sustainability. When a receiving spike comes after multiple stops and years in the wilderness, it’s fair to wonder how sticky it really is. In 2025, McGowan transferred again — this time to Kentucky. There, he carried the ball 165 times, recorded a 9.1% reception share, and posted a career-best 1.22 adjusted total yards per team play. McGowan is a big, physical back who plays through contact. Among this year’s Combine invitees, he ranks top-five in career missed tackles forced per attempt, per PFF. His explosive run rate is average at best, but when you pair the tackle-breaking ability with an acceptable receiving profile, you can at least envision a path where he sticks as a quality backup with contingent upside.

Matthew Golden.lateround_profile: There were plenty of red flags in Matthew Golden’s prospect profile. For a first-round pick, his Breakout Score wasn’t particularly strong, and his best single-season yards per route run mark was just 2.10 — a bottom-10 figure in last year’s draft class. Coming out of his rookie season, things didn’t improve. Golden’s ZAP score was buoyed by strong draft capital, but it still wasn’t anything to write home about. During the regular season, he totaled just 361 receiving yards on 267 routes run and averaged only 5.3 PPR points per game. We’re still not writing home. You could argue that Golden would’ve looked better had Green Bay simply used him more. His 1.35 yards per route run average isn’t impressive, but we’ve seen worse — including from players who eventually turned into useful fantasy assets. It’s the combination of all of these factors that makes Golden such a scary investment. Among all Year 2 Model wide receivers who failed to reach 7 PPR points per game as rookies, only seven went on to average at least 14 PPR points per game in either Year 2, 3, or 4. Two of those wideouts were Randall Cobb and Chris Godwin, and both posted yards per route run rates north of 2.0 as rookies. The peripherals were there to support future production. That leaves five names: Davante Adams, Corey Davis, DJ Chark, Nico Collins, and Jameson Williams. Davis and Chark were never consistent WR2 assets in fantasy football, and Jameson Williams only missed that mark as a rookie because of injury. So realistically, we’re talking about Davante Adams and Nico Collins. Sure, a bounce-back outcome exists for Golden. It’s just not a likely one. And in a game of probability, that’s obviously important. Golden’s Journey Comparables reinforce the concern. The published comps — Justin Hunter, KJ Hamler, and Adonai Mitchell — aren’t exactly confidence-inducing. And if you zoom out beyond those names, the picture gets even bleaker: Zay Jones, Cordarrelle Patterson, Anthony Miller, Marvin Mims, Dante Pettis, Skyy Moore, Paul Richardson. Davante Adams does appear in the behind-the-scenes comp list, but as we’ve already established, that’s not the outcome you should be betting on. The market still has some interest in Golden, but it’s understandable if you’d rather just wait this thing out at this point. The Packers’ wide receiver room could look different in 2026 as well, which only adds to the intrigue. Just understand this: the odds aren’t in his favor.

KC Concepcion.lateround_profile: Jordyn Tyson, Carnell Tate, and Makai Lemon are getting most of the “Big 3” attention in this class, but don’t overlook KC Concepcion. Unlike the more prominent names, Concepcion brings legitimate gadget work to the table. And that’s not a knock. He spent two seasons at NC State before transferring to Texas A&M for his final year, and across those three seasons, he carried the ball 70 times for 431 yards. That production gives him a modest bump in the ZAP Model. And it’s not as though the receiving work was weak. Concepcion posted a 53.9 Breakout Score, a 76th percentile number and fifth-best in the class. The score isn’t higher because he never truly had a crazy season — his best mark in receiving yards per team pass attempt was 2.25, right around the class average. But he contributed early and consistently, which the model rewards. Concepcion isn’t huge, but he compensates with elite quickness and separation ability. Like Makai Lemon, Concepcion logged heavy slot usage throughout his collegiate journey — over 70% of his routes came from the slot. But, like Lemon, Concepcion’s yards per route run rate was actually better on the perimeter than inside. That trend became clearer after his transfer. According to PFF, Concepcion’s slot rate dropped from 93% and 82% at NC State to just 34% at Texas A&M in 2025. And his yards per route run peaked during that lower-slot season. You love to see it. There’s a chance an NFL team initially views Concepcion as a slot-heavy weapon. But the data suggests he’s capable of more than that. His movement skills and perimeter effectiveness hint at a broader deployment. If the draft capital works out — particularly if he lands solidly in Round 1 — there’s little reason to believe he can’t grow into a legitimate fantasy asset. Concepcion may not carry the same ceiling narrative as the “Big 3,” but analytically, most of the boxes are checked.

Germie Bernard.lateround_profile: The ZAP Model likes Germie Bernard’s versatility. Last season at Alabama, he frequently aligned in the backfield, finishing with 18 carries for over 100 rushing yards. But Bernard didn’t spend all four collegiate seasons in Tuscaloosa. He began at Michigan State in 2022, catching just four passes as a freshman, before transferring to Washington in 2023. There, he joined a loaded receiver room featuring Rome Odunze, Jalen McMillan, Ja’Lynn Polk, and fellow 2026 Combine invite Denzel Boston. Impressively, Bernard carved out a role anyway. As a sophomore, he caught 34 passes from Michael Penix and added 13 carries for 43 yards and 2 scores. He out-targeted Boston 44 to 7 that season. Per PFF, Bernard posted a 1.90 yards per route run mark compared to Boston’s 0.89. After 2023, Bernard transferred again, finishing his career at Alabama. There, he cleared the 2.0 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark just once prior to strength-of-schedule adjustments. His Breakout Score settled at 40.4 — a 51st percentile figure. Solid, but not dominant. Given the variety of ways Bernard was deployed, it’s no surprise some of his touches were manufactured. That’s not inherently negative — coaches were clearly trying to get him the ball — but there’s risk baked into that archetype. Since 2020, 15 Day 2 wide receivers have seen 20% or more of their targets come from behind the line of scrimmage, like Bernard. The only clear hits (or projected hits) from that group are Rashee Rice, Wan’Dale Robinson, Ladd McConkey, and Luther Burden. The rest have largely disappointed. NFL Mock Draft Database projects Bernard as a Day 2 pick. Historically, receivers drafted in that range who receive the rushing bonus in the model average about one additional PPR point per game across their first three seasons compared to those who don’t. The concern lies in the efficiency. Bernard’s 1.90 career yards per route run is below the class average, and recent second- and third-round picks with sub-2.00 marks have struggled. Since 2020, Day 2 wide receivers in that bucket have underperformed their draft-capital-based B2S expectation by 1.6 PPR points per game. Receivers like Bernard often need the right ecosystem. They’re rarely plug-and-play dominators, but in creative offenses, they can become useful chess pieces. Bernard isn’t built on overwhelming production. But the versatility — and his ability to carve out roles in crowded rooms — keeps him somewhat intriguing. Though his comps leave a lot to be desired.

Kenyon Sadiq.lateround_profile: Kenyon Sadiq is the consensus top tight end prospect in the 2026 class, and his NFL Combine performance solidified it — even with competition from the TE2, Eli Stowers. Tight ends as a whole tested lighter and faster than most classes at the Combine; even with that, Sadiq is pretty light — but also really fast. He makes up for his smaller frame by posting game-breaking athleticism that featured the fastest 40-yard dash ever for a tight end at the Combine (4.39 seconds), the second-best vertical leap (43.5 inches), and the third-best broad jump (133 inches). With such a highly touted prospect (his draft capital projection is trending toward the middle of the first round after the Combine), we need to consider nitpicking a bit. If we do that, we can point out that it’s not all green across the board for the Oregon Duck. Sadiq spent three seasons at Oregon after earning a four-star recruit status coming out of high school. He got off to a slow start in a crowded offense that featured Bucky Irving and Jordan James at running back, Troy Franklin and Tez Johnson at receiver, and Bo Nix under center. As a freshman, Sadiq caught just 5 passes for 24 yards. In 2024, with Irving and Franklin gone and Dillon Gabriel leading the offense, Sadiq’s role expanded; he was fifth on the Ducks in yards (308) and seventh in receptions (24). In 2025 — and playing with his third quarterback (Dante Moore) in as many years — Sadiq led the Ducks in receptions (51) and receiving touchdowns (8) and was second in yards (560). That’s growth, and once accounting for age, the Breakout Score is still great (86th percentile). Although Sadiq took three seasons to hit his stride, he had plenty of competition in college from future NFL prospects. He’s now a virtual lock to be drafted early, typically a great sign for tight ends who go on to be high-level NFL producers. The tape shows some good blocking ability, which should make the transition more seamless for Sadiq. Early draft capital and not being a blocking liability should raise the floor on Sadiq in the NFL. He shouldn’t be used as an inline tight end if teams want to maximize his receiving anyway, so as far as a fantasy football evaluation goes, Sadiq stands out from a lot of historical prospects. It would just be nice if his comparables were a little stronger — the profile has strong odds to hit, but we’ve seen some busts from this type of player before.
