Ashton Jeanty.lateround_profile: Ashton Jeanty scored a perfect 100 in the updated ZAP Model, something only Christian McCaffrey, Saquon Barkley, and Jahmyr Gibbs have also done. And yet, you could argue — fairly convincingly — that Jeanty had the worst rookie season of the four. Among 48 qualified running backs last year, Jeanty ranked dead last in both success rate and EPA per rush, per Next Gen Stats. He was 43rd in yards per carry. That’s scary. But let me calm your fears: it doesn’t really matter. Running back production is driven heavily by situation in a given year, and Jeanty’s situation was brutal. Among backs with at least 100 carries, Quinshon Judkins was the only one, per PFF, to see fewer yards before contact per attempt than Jeanty. Unsurprisingly, Las Vegas’ offensive line ranked 30th in PFF’s run-blocking grades. With Year 2 backs, volume is the signal we care about most — it tells us the team wanted to feature the player. Jeanty had that signal. The Year 2 Model uses a weighted opportunity formula to capture workload, and Jeanty’s volume score ranks 10th-best in the database. He saw meaningful work both as a rusher and receiver, which carries strong predictive value for future seasons. Especially when paired with a perfect prospect score. Running back ZAP scores are actually more predictive of future success than Year 1 PPR points per game. Jeanty’s 14.5 PPR points per game wasn’t disastrous — it simply fell short of lofty expectations. The underlying profile still does the heavy lifting. If there’s one area worth monitoring, it’s his big-play rate. Jeanty recorded a 20-plus- yard run on just 1.1% of his carries last season. Among the six other running backs to reach Legendary Performer status in the Year 2 Model, only Christian McCaffrey failed to clear a 3% big-play rate. That’s at least mildly concerning. Still, Jeanty’s comparables couldn’t be any stronger. And yes, it’s a little uncomfortable seeing his name alongside those guys after a relatively underwhelming rookie campaign. I understand that hesitation. But this is where trusting the process matters. You don’t have to rank him as the RB1 in dynasty. You don’t even need to push him aggressively above his current market price. But you should feel confident about Jeanty’s three-year outlook — especially with the change to the coaching staff and, ideally, the quarterback position.

Isaac TeSlaa.lateround_profile: Among all rookie wide receivers since 2011 who scored six or more touchdowns, Isaac TeSlaa did it on the fewest targets (27). He barely edged out… Mecole Hardman. The whole “all he does is score touchdowns” narrative with TeSlaa is cute, but it’s not especially predictive. In fact, if you look at the rookie wideouts over the past 15 or so years who rank best in touchdowns per target (minimum six scores), the list is… not great: TeSlaa, Martavis Bryant, Hardman, Jalen McMillan, Anthony Miller, Jahan Dotson, Gabe Davis. Christian Watson, Ja’Marr Chase, and Mike Evans converted efficiently, too, but touchdown scoring alone isn’t a particularly meaningful piece of the puzzle. Volume is. And efficiency on that volume is. TeSlaa had neither in 2025. Yes, TeSlaa faced serious competition in Detroit with Amon-Ra St. Brown and Jameson Williams, but he still finished the year with a 0.81 yards per route run rate. In the Year 2 Model database, there are 21 wide receivers who ran at least 200 routes as rookies and posted a sub-0.90 yards per route run figure. Only one of them — Nelson Agholor — ever averaged more than 10 PPR points per game in Year 2, 3, or 4. And even then, Agholor was more waiver-wire filler than fantasy cornerstone. TeSlaa is a fun story, and he’s a fun player to watch — he throws his body around like Puka Nacua does. But it would be beyond shocking if he followed in Puka Nacua’s footsteps. Even 50% of Nacua’s production would be a win.

RJ Harvey.lateround_profile: We should always take single-season running back efficiency metrics with a grain of salt. They’re not overly predictive, and it’s easy to overstate their importance. With that being said, poor marks still matter. And RJ Harvey had some in 2025. Among 48 qualified backs, Harvey ranked 46th in rushing yards over expected per attempt, per Next Gen Stats. His success rate was 32nd — notably well behind teammate JK Dobbins, who ranked 12th in the same offensive environment. On the positive side, Harvey’s burst — one of his strongest traits entering the league — did show up at times. His 109.4 Speed Score translated into a 2.1% rate of 20-plus-yard runs. Only 55 other backs in the Year 2 Model database have cleared that 2% threshold on 100-plus rookie carries. The issue is that the explosiveness wasn’t consistent. While he connected on a few long gains, Harvey’s 5.5% rate of 10-plus-yard runs ranked sixth-worst all-time among Year 2 Model backs with 100-plus first-year carries. In other words, the home runs were there — the steady stream of chunk plays wasn’t. We also saw a solid 10% target share, and he was efficient enough through the air. That’s meaningful in a Sean Payton offense that has historically featured running backs as receivers. That part of his profile carries real weight for long-term fantasy value. And, of course, there were the touchdowns. Harvey scored 12 total times — rare for a rookie back. But touchdowns are far less reliable than prospect scores, early-career volume, and core efficiency metrics. While plenty of rookie backs with double-digit totals went on to strong careers, the list also includes players like Antonio Gibson, James Robinson, Eddie Lacy, and Alfred Morris — productive, but not long-term fantasy cornerstones. The fact that Harvey didn’t fully seize the starting job until Dobbins was injured is at least mildly concerning, though it’s not uncommon for rookies to require time before a takeover. The larger concern is that his rushing efficiency never truly separated. So, where do we go from here? Well, Dobbins re-signed in Denver, which creates somewhat of a roadblock for Harvey’s short-term outlook. But honestly, it was still one of the better outcomes for the second-year player in free agency. Denver could’ve landed a bigger name like Kenneth Walker, after all. The Broncos may add another back in the draft, but at least for Harvey’s sake, this year’s class isn’t particularly strong. From that perspective, he may end up in an OK spot. Harvey’s age is a concern — he’s already 25 — but if you set proper expectations, you should be able to navigate his market. He’s capable of delivering an RB2 season over the next few years, but the ceiling is likely capped. He, to me, is not a bell-cow back.

Kaleb Johnson.lateround_profile: Do we really have to talk about Kaleb Johnson? Each year, as you know, the ZAP Model gets refined. New data is tested. New inputs are introduced. Player scores shift. This current iteration of the running back ZAP Model is the best it’s ever been. And it still likes Kaleb Johnson. Great. Johnson had arguably the most disappointing rookie season of any back in last year’s class. From a volume standpoint alone, he joins 19 other Year 2 Model running backs drafted inside the top 100 who failed to reach 50 rush attempts as rookies. On his 28 carries, he didn’t record a single 10-plus-yard run. We’re off to a great start. When we isolate backs drafted between Picks 32 and 100 since 2011 who failed to reach 50 rookie rush attempts, there have been a few spike seasons. Six produced at least 13.0 PPR points per game in one of Years 2 through 4. One of those was Mike Leshoure, who tore his Achilles before his rookie season and never really had the opportunity for that workload. The others? Shane Vereen, Kenyan Drake, Ronald Jones, Damien Harris, and Darrell Henderson. There’s a path to some one-off production, as that group of backs shows. But for Johnson to become a true fantasy staple, he’d essentially have to break historical precedent for backs in his draft range over the last decade-plus. You can make that bet if you’d like — especially with his dynasty price cratered. There’s a new coaching staff in Pittsburgh, and while the Steelers did add Rico Dowdle (while losing Kenneth Gainwell), you never know how a new set of coaches will want to run things. But let’s be realistic: Kaleb Johnson profiles more like this year’s Ja’Lynn Polk than a misunderstood breakout candidate. It’s probably not going to happen. The one sliver of hope? Running back prospect scores tend to carry more rebound value than wide receiver scores after a poor rookie season. So while the odds aren’t close to being in his favor, the position itself at least leaves the door cracked open.

Emmanuel Henderson.lateround_profile: Among the 46 wide receivers invited to this year’s NFL Combine, Emmanuel Henderson ranked second with a 40.5% career deep-ball target rate. He was also fourth in collegiate average depth of target. What’s interesting is that Henderson wasn’t strictly a boundary-only player. He ran about 36% of his routes from the slot, which is a fairly healthy rate for someone with that kind of downfield profile. Henderson’s college path took him to Alabama for three seasons before transferring to Kansas for his final year. At Alabama, there was essentially no meaningful receiving production — he caught just five passes across three seasons. His final year there resulted in an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate of 0.28. Kansas is where things finally clicked. In 2025, he caught 45 passes for 766 yards and 5 touchdowns, posting a 2.39 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt. That was enough to get him on the board with a Breakout Score, but it was just 21.1. So we’ve got one productive season, and it came late. Henderson also contributed in the return game, bringing back 18 kicks for 455 yards and a touchdown in his final season. That special teams juice could play a role in NFL outlook. But from a fantasy perspective, this is a historically fragile archetype. Since 2020, 17 wide receivers have entered the league with a deep-ball target rate of 35% or higher. Henderson was north of 40%. None of those 17 averaged 10 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons. When a player’s offensive profile is heavily tilted toward vertical shots, it usually signals a limited route tree and volatile usage. Henderson could carve out a role as a speed-based rotational receiver and special teams contributor. That can absolutely help an NFL team. Fantasy managers, though, shouldn’t expect much beyond the occasional splash play.

Dylan Sampson.lateround_profile: Dylan Sampson finished 2025 with an 8.6% target share. That may not sound like much, but get this: among Year 2 Model running backs selected outside the top 100 picks, only 21 have reached an 8% target share as rookies. What makes Sampson’s season even more unusual is that he didn’t enter the league with a strong collegiate receiving résumé. His best prorated reception share in college was under 10%. Of those 21 rookie backs to hit an 8% NFL target share, only seven had a sub-10% reception share in college. Unfortunately for Sampson managers, that subset hasn’t produced much fantasy upside. None of those backs became true difference-makers after Year 1. The best outcome was probably Jordan Howard, who topped out at 12.8 PPR points per game post-rookie season. Maybe there’s a small silver lining: Sampson was efficient as a receiver, posting the highest yards-per-route-run rate among that group. Maybe that matters enough to keep the momentum going into Year 2 and beyond. Maybe. On the ground, though, little stood out — and what did wasn’t encouraging. Sampson became just the ninth Year 2 Model back with 50-plus rookie carries to post a sub-5% 10-plus-yard run rate. While he managed a few 20-plus-yard gains, the down-to-down consistency wasn’t there. His 29.1% success rate was borderline alarming. You may have read earlier that Quinshon Judkins ranked last in yards before contact per attempt this past year. But that was when looking at higher-volume backs. When loosening that filter a bit and looking at guys with 50 or more carries, Sampson actually ranks even worse — he’s dead last in the league by a healthy margin. To put that another way, it’s likely that Sampson’s line wasn’t doing him any favors this past year. Still, the overall profile doesn’t scream high ceiling. If Quinshon Judkins is healthy, Sampson isn’t leading this backfield. He’s a capable handcuff — and his receiving usage supports that — but projecting much more than that in the short term feels aggressive. Down the line, perhaps his top Journey Comparable of Chuba Hubbard offers a plausible path. But that’s more developmental outcome than immediate expectation. Jacory Croskey-Merritt • RB The hype around Jacory Croskey-Merritt was very real just a week or two before the 2025 season, and he backed it up early. After the first month-plus, Next Gen Stats had Croskey- Merritt — “Bill” — posting the best success rate in football. Washington was still splitting the backfield, but Bill was undeniably productive. Then regression hit. Four of Croskey-Merritt’s first five games featured a success rate of at least 50%. During that stretch, he averaged a ridiculous 2.2 rushing yards over expected per attempt and gained 10 or more yards on nearly 21% of his carries. After Week 5, the numbers cooled significantly. Croskey-Merritt had just two games with a 50% success rate the rest of the way. His rushing yards over expected per attempt fell to 0.34, and his 10-plus-yard run rate dropped to 9.1%. Even so, his full-season body of work isn’t terrible. Bill generated a 10-plus-yard run at a 12% clip — above average within the class — and finished with 8.6 PPR points per game. His comparables across those metrics include Jerick McKinnon, Tyjae Spears, Tarik Cohen, and Keaton Mitchell. The issue? Most of those players were meaningfully involved as receivers early in their careers. Croskey-Merritt wasn’t in Year 1. He finished with just a 3.0% target share and ran a route on only 25% of Washington’s dropbacks. Woof. Overall, Croskey-Merritt’s rushing profile fits that of a big-play complementary back. The problem is that there’s little evidence of a receiving backbone — even in college, despite more than four seasons of play, he never reached a 10% reception share. That could explain why the Commanders went out and signed the pass-catching Rachaad White. Bill should continue to have a role in Washington’s backfield, though. It may not be a massive one, but it’ll be something. But absent growth in the passing game, it’s difficult to project him as anything more than a rotational option rather than a featured piece.

Jack Endries.lateround_profile: Jack Endries has a strong tie to the 2025 Heisman Trophy winner, Fernando Mendoza. They played two seasons together in 2023 and 2024 at Cal before transferring to different schools. Endries, as a redshirt sophomore, led the Golden Bears in receptions (56) and yards (623) before transferring to Texas, where his yards per game number (26.6) was nearly cut in half (47.9) amidst tougher competition despite starting all 11 games for the Longhorns. Endries, in total, rates out with strong max yards per team pass attempt data (67th percentile) and Breakout Score (73rd percentile) despite raw yards-per-route rate numbers that may not grab your attention too much. He also ran a 4.62-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine, giving him a height-adjusted Speed Score of 116.0 at 6’5”, 245. On the flip side, despite a smaller frame and good athletic testing, Endries doesn’t pop from a yards-after-the-catch standpoint. That’s also tied to some average-to-below-average blocking scores from PFF and SportsInfoSolutions (and it’s worth noting that he graded out with around 15th-percentile arm length). After struggling with drops in his first season with Cal, his hands improved markedly in his final two seasons. That’s promising growth. He’s the exact type of player (decent athleticism for his size with questionable blocking traits but who might still be drafted early anyway) that the new tight end model finds interesting. His draft stock will greatly affect his ZAP score, as it’ll tell us a lot about how NFL teams are valuing his strengths compared to his weaknesses. That’s the case for every tight end outside the top two. Endries has a pretty wide range of pre-draft valuations, spanning from the middle of the second round to one of the first names being called on Day 3. If a team likes him and spends legitimate Day 2 capital on him, he becomes pretty interesting. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 111111

Tetairoa McMillan.lateround_profile: Tetairoa McMillan wants to be called T-Mac. Just before interviewing him on Radio Row back in early February, his reps approached us and stressed that point. T-Mac. Nothing but T-Mac. And honestly, I’ll call him whatever he wants after his fantastic rookie season. The man balled out. McMillan — sorry, T-Mac — finished his rookie campaign with a 25.4% target share and a 1.84 yards-per-route-run rate. Since 2011, the only rookie wide receivers to clear both a 25% target share and a 1.80 yards-per-route-run mark are Odell Beckham, Mike Evans, Puka Nacua, Justin Jefferson, Brian Thomas, Malik Nabers, Drake London, Chris Olave, and… Kelvin Benjamin. That’s a lot of insane wide receiver talent. And Kelvin Benjamin. Looking at first-down share, T-Mac clears again. His per-game first-down share sat at 32.4% last season, the fourth-highest mark among all wide receivers in the Year 2 Model database. The three players ahead of him? Malik Nabers, Odell Beckham Jr., and Puka Nacua. Pretty great company! T-Mac averaged just 12.4 PPR points per game thanks to a below-average catchable target rate and an offense that ranked in the bottom five in pass rate over expected. Those two factors could limit his ceiling a bit moving forward – Bryce Young isn’t the most complete quarterback, and Dave Canales leans toward a run-centric offense. Over his two years in Carolina, the Panthers have had below-average pass rate over expected numbers. That’s the thing that might stop T-Mac. Because from a talent perspective, McMillan has the tools to be a true difference-maker at the next level. His Journey Comparables of A.J. Brown and Justin Jefferson underscore that point. If you’ve got McMillan on your dynasty squad, you should feel great about it. The ceiling is high if the circumstances surrounding him improve. Even if they don’t, he should still be very usable.

J. Michael Sturdivant.lateround_profile: We actually did see a flicker of early-career promise from J. Michael Sturdivant, the least user-friendly database name in the class (if you know, you know). Sturdivant began at Cal, and in his second season there, he posted an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark that barely missed the (low) Breakout Score threshold. Unfortunately, that ended up being the high point of his collegiate journey in that metric, so he never officially recorded a Breakout Score. After Cal, Sturdivant transferred to UCLA for two seasons and then finished at Florida. The cumulative profile is rough: a career 1.40 yards per route run, fourth-worst in this class. Over the last five years, no wide receiver in the ZAP Model database with a sub- 1.50 career yards per route run and no Breakout Score has produced more than 6.0 PPR points per game in a season. There are legitimate physical tools. At 6’3”, 207 pounds, Sturdivant ran a 4.40 40-yard dash. His Speed Score is excellent, and his broad and vertical jumps were strong as well. On paper, that’s a really appealing athletic foundation. If those measurables belonged to a 21-year-old prospect, you could talk yourself into a developmental upside case. But Sturdivant will be over 23-and-a-half at draft time. At that age, with that age-adjusted production, it’s a difficult sell. The physical tools are there. The track record isn’t. And historically, that combination — especially for an older prospect — hasn’t led to fantasy relevance. Jonah Coleman.lateround_profile: Jonah Coleman is a true do-it-all back. Coleman began his collegiate career at Arizona, logging over 200 combined carries in his freshman and sophomore seasons. After transferring to Washington for his final two years, he elevated his profile even further. Coleman peaked in total yards per team play as a junior, though that season featured a reception share under 8%. As a senior, he brought everything together, posting a similar yards-per-team-play mark while nearly doubling his prorated reception share to 13.2%. Stylistically, Coleman profiles as a powerful workhorse back. According to PFF, no running back in this class posted a better missed tackles forced per attempt rate. He also ranked top-five in yards after contact per attempt and earned one of the strongest pass-blocking grades among NFL Combine invitees. Some evaluators question his long speed, but his career explosive run rate sits above the class average. He’s not a pure burner, but he’s not devoid of big-play ability, either. If Coleman lands on Day 2, his outlook meaningfully improves. Since 2011, the ZAP Model database includes 39 running backs drafted after Round 1 but inside the top 100 who weighed at least 215 pounds. Of the 34 who’ve played at least three NFL seasons, 18 posted a best-season reception share above 10%. The other 16 did not. The higher-reception group outperformed their draft-capital-based expectation (via B2S) by 4.2 PPR points per game. The lower-reception group underperformed by 1.3 points per contest. Coleman’s senior-year receiving profile places him on the right side of that split — assuming the draft capital cooperates. And draft capital will matter here. If he hears his name called inside the top 100, you’re looking at a well above-average prospect with size, tackle-breaking ability, pass-blocking competence, and a growing receiving résumé. That’d be a profile worth betting on.

Pat Bryant.lateround_profile: For what looked like an overdrafted Day 2 selection by the Broncos, Pat Bryant’s rookie season could’ve been worse. But make no mistake — it wasn’t fantastic. Beyond the raw numbers, Bryant posted just 1.22 yards per route run and recorded a first down on a little over 7% of his routes. He also failed to reach 5.0 PPR points per game, settling in at 4.8. Those results aren’t shocking for a Round 3 pick, but there wasn’t much to get excited about with Bryant during most of the 2025 season. If there’s one positive to point to, it’s how his role — and efficiency — changed as the year went on. From Weeks 1 through 8, Bryant saw just a 36% route share and averaged 3.6 PPR points per game. According to PFF data, his yards per route run during that stretch was an abysmal 0.48. Then things shifted. As the Broncos began to scale back Troy Franklin’s usage, Bryant’s route share jumped from 36% to 69% for the remainder of the regular season. His PPR points per game increased by four, and more importantly, his yards per route run climbed from 0.48 to a very respectable 1.58. Courtland Sutton will turn 31 in October, and while Troy Franklin has proven to be a solid contributor, he hasn’t shown himself to be an elite one. There’s a path for Bryant to continue carving out a larger role in Denver’s offense given his second-half surge. And because the dynasty market largely views Bryant as an afterthought, he’s the type of player worth throwing a dart at. The floor is low, but at this cost, seeing what he’s got in 2026 makes plenty of sense.

Noah Whittington.lateround_profile: Noah Whittington played six seasons of college football and never reached 1,000 total yards in a single year. Whittington’s most encouraging stretch likely came in Year 3, his first season at Oregon after two years at Western Kentucky. That year, he posted his best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate and his second-best adjusted total yards per team play mark. He also tied his career-high reception share at 7.1%. And he did that in the same offense as Bucky Irving. You’d hope the arrow would continue pointing up from there, but it didn’t. Whittington tore his ACL in 2023, the following season, and the profile never really recovered. His best-season reception share ranked bottom-five in the class. His adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was also bottom-five. His top adjusted total yards per team play mark — 1.20 — ranked third-worst among Combine-invited running backs. Even if we soften the thresholds by 20% — pushing them to roughly an 8.5% reception share, 1.45 total yards per team play, and 0.50 receiving yards per team pass attempt — Whittington still falls well short. In the ZAP Model database, 66 running backs have failed to meet those boosted baselines. Just two — Andre Ellington and Chris Carson — produced even one fantasy-relevant season in their first three years. That’s not encouraging. The profile is iffy, and the lack of any true ceiling season makes it difficult to sell Whittington as anything more than a depth guy. As always with running backs, landing spot can manufacture short-term opportunity. But analytically, this is not a profile you’d want to aggressively bet on.

Jaylin Noel.lateround_profile: My subjective take is that the Year 2 Model is probably a little too low on Jaylin Noel. Objectively, though, his score makes sense. The Texans barely deployed Noel in 2025 — he didn’t have a single game as a rookie with a 50% route share. It’s difficult for any player to project well in a forecasting model when the volume is that limited. As expected, Noel was used primarily out of the slot as a rookie, just as he was at Iowa State. Houston deployed him in a very similar fashion, too. His average depth of target sat at just 9.1 yards, but nearly a quarter of his targets came on throws of 20 or more air yards. That aDOT was suppressed largely because 24% of his targets also came behind the line of scrimmage, one of the higher rates in the league. And Noel does have downfield ability. He showed it in college, and as a rookie, he caught six of his eight deep targets, per PFF data. In fact, Noel finished second on the Texans in receptions on throws of 20-plus air yards, trailing only Nico Collins. Where Noel may struggle – at least from a fantasy perspective — is in the intermediate area of the field. And that’s often a valuable place for fantasy production. Targets in the 10- to-19 air-yard window are likely to funnel toward Collins and Jayden Higgins, which could leave Noel with fewer of those high-leverage opportunities. Still, Noel finished the season with a respectable 1.45 yards per route run and managed three double-digit PPR performances despite his limited role. If free agent Christian Kirk is out of Houston in 2026, Noel could carve out a slightly larger piece of the offense. That role just isn’t likely to be as robust as we’d ideally want for fantasy purposes. Still, he’s a good player in my eyes.

Khalil Dinkins.lateround_profile: Khalil Dinkins is expected to go undrafted despite a 6’4”, 251-pound frame. Dinkins capped out at 14 catches and 167 yards in a single season at Penn State, and no matter how you slice the numbers, he’s toward the bottom of the tight end database in terms of adjusted production. He did post some good size-adjusted agility data at the Combine, but the explosiveness results were below-average, and the height-adjusted Speed Score (based on his 4.72-second 40-yard dash) is just about average. Dinkins also repped 225 on the bench 25 times, which is over the threshold where we tend to see a signal that the given tight end is more of a blocking tight end than a receiving tight end. He could find a spot on an NFL roster as a big-bodied, blocking tight end with some situational pass-catching opportunities, but at the end of the day, the receiving profile is too pedestrian to ignore for Dinkins when we’re seeking pure fantasy football upside. Travis Hunter.lateround_profile: Travis Hunter will go down as one of the most difficult prospect evaluations I’ve ever had. The ZAP Model was never particularly fond of him, and the updated version likes him even less. That’s largely because his collegiate analytical profile simply wasn’t anything to write home about. You can excuse some of that given Hunter’s two-way role at Colorado, but that versatility only added more uncertainty to his projection. Still, thanks to his draft capital, he was slotted into the top tier of last year’s rookie drafts. At one point, it looked like Hunter might justify that investment. He appeared to be on the verge of becoming Jacksonville’s top target earner before an LCL injury cut his rookie season short. That injury forced the Jaguars’ front office to act, and they responded by acquiring veteran wide receiver Jakobi Meyers at the deadline. Hunter’s LCL may end up having longer-term fantasy implications. Jacksonville ultimately gave Meyers a new deal, locking him in through at least the 2027 season. While Hunter was sidelined, Parker Washington emerged as a viable slot option. And Brian Thomas remains entrenched on the outside with multiple years left on his rookie contract. (We’ll see if that changes or not.) So where does that leave Hunter in this offense? The Jaguars don’t have to force the issue with him. And analytically speaking, it’s fair to ask whether they should. Nothing from Hunter’s 2025 season meaningfully challenges his ZAP score. A 1.32 yards-per-route-run rate is underwhelming, and Jacksonville didn’t target him at a particularly high rate when he was on the field — just 20% of his routes run saw a target. The breakout felt like it might be coming, but we can’t say with confidence that it ever truly arrived. Add in reporting from ESPN suggesting that Jacksonville views Hunter primarily as a cornerback with situational offensive packages, and the outlook becomes even murkier. As a dynasty manager, that’s a tough pill to swallow. In the short term — especially looking ahead to 2026 — the Jaguars have legitimate offensive options at wide receiver, and all signs point toward Hunter settling into something less than a lead role. Hunter was more of a sell earlier in the offseason when the reporting wasn’t as obvious about his role — his value on Keep Trade Cut has fallen by a decent bit since January. Now, you may have to wait for positive news to make any sort of sell move.

Jam Miller.lateround_profile: Jam Miller flashed some juice at the NFL Combine. At 209 pounds, he ran a 4.42 40, giving him a Speed Score just shy of 110. That might be the best part of the profile, if we’re being honest. Miller spent four seasons at Alabama and never reached 150 carries in a year. His best rush attempt share season topped out at 29.7%. Through the air, he never hit an 8% reception share in any campaign. That combination — modest workload and minimal receiving — is historically tough to overcome. There are 34 running backs in the ZAP Model database who failed to clear a 30% best-season attempt share and an 8% reception share. Just four of them reached 10 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons: CJ Anderson, Chris Carson, Dameon Pierce, and Damien Harris. All four weighed at least 215 pounds entering the league. Miller doesn’t. Even if we loosen the thresholds — backs under 215 pounds with a best-season attempt share below 40% and a best-season reception share below 10% — there are 43 players in that bucket. Only Devonta Freeman, Elijah Mitchell, and Darrell Henderson produced meaningful fantasy seasons. And that’s before diving into the advanced rushing metrics. Despite the strong 40 time, Miller ranked dead last in this year’s class in collegiate explosive run rate, per PFF. It’s one of the lowest marks we’ve seen in the last five draft cycles. His yards after contact per attempt also ranked dead last. Alabama’s depth chart is never easy to crack, and they obviously play some tough defenses. But the overall résumé doesn’t suggest we should be pounding the table for Miller this offseason. The Speed Score is intriguing. The production profile is not.

Jeff Caldwell.lateround_profile: Jeff Caldwell might be the biggest size-speed outlier in this entire class. He’s 6’5”, 216 pounds, and ran a 4.31 at the Combine. On top of that, he posted the second-best broad jump and vertical among wide receiver invitees. That’s absurd movement at that frame. Those testing numbers probably shifted him from fringe-undrafted territory into “someone will take a shot” range. But Caldwell’s outlook is almost entirely traits-based. Caldwell began his career at Lindenwood in 2022, and he stayed there for three seasons. On the surface, a 3.24 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark in 2024 looks impressive. Once adjusted for strength of competition, though, it falls below 2.0. We have to always remember that raw dominance at a lower level doesn’t carry the same signal. Caldwell transferred to Cincinnati for his final season and finished with 32 catches for 478 yards and 6 touchdowns. His 1.31 receiving yards per team pass attempt there didn’t move the needle analytically. The result is a 29th percentile Breakout Score and a best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt of 1.94. That’s not the kind of production profile that typically overcomes mid-to-late-round draft capital. However, there’s no denying the athletic profile. Caldwell’s one of the best athletes we’ve seen come out of the Combine. It’s rare. But historically, betting on testing without meaningful, translatable production is tough. Unless draft capital comes in far stronger than expected, Caldwell is more of a last-round rookie draft dart throw than someone you need to prioritize.

Kaytron Allen.lateround_profile: When you first look at Kaytron Allen’s production profile, the lack of receiving output immediately jumps out. His best-season prorated reception share was just 8.6%, sixth-worst in the class, and his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt sat at 0.50 — also sixth-worst. Allen spent all four collegiate seasons at Penn State alongside fellow 2026 Combine invite Nick Singleton. Singleton absorbed more of the passing-game work, which naturally suppresses Allen’s receiving résumé in the model. Teammate Score helps offset that context, but the raw receiving numbers still matter. Stylistically, Allen was the thunder to Singleton’s lightning. He’s a bruising, downhill runner who posted a stronger missed tackles forced per attempt rate than his teammate. That said, his tackle-breaking numbers were fairly ordinary relative to the rest of this year’s class, and his career yards after contact per attempt was similarly average. When you combine grind-it-out rushing metrics with limited receiving involvement, the profile can look concerning. And historically, it often is. The ZAP Model database includes more than 170 running backs who entered the league with a sub-10% best-season prorated reception share and a peak adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt below 0.60 — Allen, again, sits at 8.6% and 0.50. Of that 170-plus group, only 7 managed to score 14 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons: Alfred Morris, Andre Ellington, CJ Anderson, Carlos Hyde, Jordan Howard, Chris Carson, and Isiah Pacheco. This is where adjusted total yards per team play becomes important. Allen was highly productive overall — in fact, he’s Penn State’s all-time leading rusher. Despite the weaker receiving marks, he posted a 1.96 adjusted total yards per team play rate. Among those seven historical “hits,” only Jordan Howard and Carlos Hyde reached that level. Add in the fact that Allen operated alongside legitimate backfield competition throughout his career, and the profile doesn’t look quite as bleak as the receiving numbers alone might suggest. There are real red flags here — particularly for true RB1 upside. But if draft capital is there, Allen has a path to carving out early-down value with contingent ceiling. It’s just a narrower path than some of his peers in this class.

Jayden Higgins.lateround_profile: The Texans were pretty infuriating with their wide receiver usage in 2025, rotating players frequently and often giving Xavier Hutchinson more snaps than rookie Jayden Higgins. As a result, Higgins finished the regular season with just a 56% route share — a figure that ranked below players like Van Jefferson and David Sills. When you include the playoffs, though, things look a little different. Higgins reached an 80% route share three times last year, and in those games, he scored 11.9, 10.3, and 13.4 PPR points. In his next-highest route share game (68%), he posted 13.8 PPR points. Maybe let him run more routes? From an efficiency standpoint, Higgins’ rookie-year metrics were perfectly respectable. His 1.45 yards per route run was fairly ordinary for a first-year wide receiver, and he was targeted on fewer than 20% of his routes. His catch rate over expected was exactly average — literally 0%. That said, Higgins was still better than both Christian Kirk and Xavier Hutchinson in Houston last season, the two wide receivers he was competing with most for snaps. He outpaced them in yards per route run, EPA per target, and catch rate over expected, while also posting an average depth of target north of 12 yards. “Solid” is probably the best word to describe Higgins’ rookie season. He was used primarily as an intermediate option, and he mostly got the job done. With Hutchinson as beatable competition and Kirk no longer on the team, there’s a legitimate path for Higgins to step into a larger role in Year 2. And that’s exciting, because Higgins’ Journey Comps are pretty interesting. They do paint a very high-variance picture, though.

Vinny Anthony.lateround_profile: Vinny Anthony spent four years at Wisconsin and topped 400 receiving yards in just one of them. That junior-season spike was enough to get him to a 33.5 Breakout Score, but that’s about where the optimism peaks. From there, the profile gets shaky. Anthony’s best single-season yards per route run mark was a respectable 2.29, but the consistency and drive-ending impact just weren’t there. Anthony gained a first down on only 6.4% of his routes in college, the fourth-worst rate in this year’s class. Historically, that’s a major red flag. Since 2020, the ZAP Model has logged 32 wide receivers drafted outside the top 100 with first downs per route run rates below 7%. Not one of them reached 10 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons. In fact, all but two failed to even hit 3.0 PPR points per game. You can get that kind of production off the waiver wire. Even in dynasty leagues. Anthony could carve out a depth role, but from a fantasy perspective, the odds of him becoming someone you’re confidently starting on Sundays are extremely slim.

Chris Brazzell.lateround_profile: The Tennessee offense hasn’t been the easiest to evaluate under Josh Heupel, and we’ve got another wideout from the program in this class: Chris Brazzell. Brazzell stands 6’4”, 198 pounds — long, lean, and built to run. He used that frame to stress defenses vertically. Among wideouts in this class, he saw deep targets at a top-15 rate, and nearly 18% of his receptions came on those throws, a top-10 figure. His career average depth of target, per PFF, ranked top-five. The usage fits the archetype. Brazzell lined up in the slot on fewer than 7% of his collegiate snaps, the lowest rate in the class. He’s a true boundary receiver whose job was to stretch the field pretty often. From a ZAP Model standpoint, the inputs are solid but not insane. After starting at Tulane, Brazzell transferred to Tennessee in 2024. His real breakout came in his final season, when he first cleared a 2.0 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. That resulted in a 45.7 Breakout Score, good for the 63rd percentile. We’ve seen this type hit before. Bigger-bodied vertical receivers taken on Day 2 in recent years — Nico Collins, Tee Higgins, George Pickens, Christian Watson — show there’s a real ceiling here. The catch is that most of those hits came with stronger efficiency profiles than Brazzell’s 2.09 career yards per route run. When you look around that threshold, you start getting into names like Denzel Mims or Cedric Tillman. That’s the tension with Brazzell. The upside is obvious. So is the volatility. And his comparables do a good job capturing that.

Kyle Williams.lateround_profile: The hype train was real for Kyle Williams last summer — and I’ll admit it, I bought in, too. Unfortunately, that train came to a screeching halt midway through the season when it became clear this just wasn’t going to happen in 2025. Williams finished the year with 10 catches. He played all 17 games, but topped a 50% route share only three times — and that came on a team that frequently rotated its wide receivers. To his credit, Williams flashed some speed. He caught three touchdowns of 30 or more yards, including a 72-yard bomb against Tampa Bay in November. That was fun. But beyond his role as a deep-ball specialist, there isn’t much in the underlying profile to get excited about. Among wide receivers with at least 150 routes, Williams ranked 11th in 20-plus air-yard target rate last season. His Journey Comparables don’t inspire much confidence, either. David Bell was a college production monster who never found footing in the NFL. Anthony Schwartz, another speed- based prospect, caught 10 passes as a rookie before totaling just four the rest of his career. And Tyler Johnson was well-liked by the ZAP Model as a Day 3 pick, but has since settled in as a journeyman wide receiver. Hopefully, Williams beats those outcomes. But the most likely path here is that he becomes a high-variance bench asset in dynasty formats.

Omarion Hampton.lateround_profile: Does the ZAP Model like Omarion Hampton a little too much? Maybe. But aside from injury, it’s not as if he flopped as a rookie. Out of 48 qualified running backs, Hampton ranked 15th in rushing yards over expected per attempt last season, per Next Gen Stats. He was 22nd in success rate — and that came behind an offensive line that graded out as the worst run-blocking unit in the league, according to PFF. It was also encouraging to see him involved as a pass-catcher. The ZAP Model viewed Hampton as a capable receiver entering the league, and in an offense (Greg Roman was offensive coordinator) that doesn’t typically feature running backs heavily through the air, he posted a target share north of 12%. From 2011 through 2022, there have been 10 running backs in the Year 2 Model database who entered the league as Legendary Performers or Elite Producers and then earned at least a 12% target share as rookies. Among those backs, only Trent Richardson and Giovani Bernard failed to provide an RB1 season within their next three campaigns. The situation in LA also appears to be trending in the right direction. The Chargers added Mike McDaniel to coordinate the offense, and both Rashawn Slater and Joe Alt are expected to be healthy in 2026. Even if Kimani Vidal’s emergence trims Hampton’s workload slightly — or even the addition of Keaton Mitchell — there’s little reason to believe Hampton can’t ascend into high-end fantasy territory as soon as this season. Yes, seeing Trent Richardson pop up as a Journey Comparable is uncomfortable. But Hampton- type profiles historically perform far better. That comp exists to keep us grounded, not to define the projection.

TreVeyon Henderson.lateround_profile: TreVeyon Henderson had a strange rookie season. We didn’t see a true breakout until Week 10, when Rhamondre Stevenson was sidelined. But when it happened, it was loud. Henderson rushed for 147 yards on 14 carries against Tampa Bay and then posted at least 80 total yards in each of the next four games. A concussion in Week 16 stalled that momentum. And even once he returned during New England’s playoff run, Stevenson handled the majority of the backfield work — Henderson logged a sub-40% snap share from the Divisional Round onward. One clear takeaway from Year 1 was Henderson’s explosiveness. He gained 20 or more yards on over 3% of his rushes, the highest rate in this rookie class. Only 33 other running backs in the Year 2 Model database have reached that threshold on 100-plus carries as rookies. Of those backs who’ve played four years in the league, they averaged a best PPR points-per- game season of 14.5 between Years 2 and 4. That’s a very solid high-end RB2 campaign. The less encouraging takeaway was in pass protection. Henderson entered the league with a reputation as an elite blocker, but that didn’t translate immediately. Among backs with 100-plus attempts in 2025, he ranked 41st out of 49 in PFF pass-blocking grade. Stevenson ranked third. That discrepancy likely played a role in Stevenson maintaining his role, especially early in the year. And to be fair, Stevenson earned it late in the season. After a rocky start, he finished strong. That allowed him to post the highest rushing yards over expected per attempt among qualified backs, per Next Gen Stats. Henderson’s receiving profile also didn’t fully materialize. An 8.7% target share isn’t poor, but for a player whose collegiate résumé suggested more pass-game involvement, that number needs to grow for him to hit his ceiling. Stevenson’s late-season surge — combined with the fact that he remains under contract in 2026 — makes Henderson’s short-term outlook a bit murky. We should expect more volume in Year 2, and his long-term upside remains substantial. But given his current market price, the direction you go with Henderson likely depends on where your team stands — a win-now roster may want to capitalize and sell, while a rebuilding one may prefer to hold. The good news? Henderson’s Journey Comparables are pretty strong.

Bhayshul Tuten.lateround_profile: At the start of the offseason — back in January — yours truly listed Bhayshul Tuten as a buy candidate. Then, a couple of weeks later, I sent a newsletter out with a copy of this very player page, telling people not to be too aggressive. It now seems like, based on general sentiment, people don’t know where I stand. So I decided to re-write this whole thing to be clear: Bhayshul Tuten is more of a buy than a sell. That feeling may not be an intense one, but that’s the side I lean. It’s no secret that the ZAP Model liked Tuten. And it’s no secret that he didn’t live up to the hype in 2025. After Tank Bigsby was dealt to Philadelphia, Travis Etienne took firm control of the Jacksonville backfield. There were moments when it felt like Tuten might carve out a larger role, but something always intervened — an injury, a hot stretch from Etienne — and Tuten would slide back into the background. Tuten’s ZAP Model profile was legitimately strong. And there were positives to his rookie season. He, for instance, posted a 47% success rate compared to Etienne’s 36%. That grinder mentality led to the Jags using Tuten frequently at the goal line — he had 7 goal-line attempts to Etienne’s 12, which is notable given Etienne’s workload advantage. Tuten also gained positive yardage on over 85% of his rushes, a top-two mark among 50-plus carry rookies from the 2025 class. One issue is that the explosiveness that fueled his prospect appeal didn’t really show up in Year 1. On 83 rush attempts, Tuten failed to record a single run of 20 or more yards (though he did hit a 20-yard run in the playoffs). He had just two runs of 15-plus yards. In the Year 2 Model database, 13 other running backs have logged at least 50 rookie carries without a 20-plus-yard run in the regular season. Of those 13, only one went on to average more than 10 PPR points per game in Years 2 through 4. The sample is small, but that’s not exactly comforting. So this, at the moment, is a high-variance profile. On one end, you have a player beloved by the ZAP Model relative to draft capital expectations. And he had some encouraging marks as a rookie. On the other, the explosiveness that we — the model — liked to see wasn’t totally evident in Year 1. Travis Etienne is gone now, though. All that’s left on the Jacksonville running back depth chart is Tuten, LeQuint Allen, and now Chris Rodriguez. Tuten should at least get some shot this year, even if it’s in tandem with Rodriguez, who hasn’t been much of a pass-catcher in college or the NFL. And I’m more on the side that Tuten’s more talented than not — we can’t just totally forget about his ZAP score.

Dillon Bell.lateround_profile: The ZAP Model database now has six wide receivers with the last name Bell. Three of them are in this 2026 class. That’s analytics. Dillon Bell is generally viewed as the least exciting of the trio. He spent four seasons at Georgia — which is never a bad line on the résumé — but he never topped 466 receiving yards in a season. His best-year receiving yards per team pass attempt was under 1.0. Before this cycle, the ZAP Model had seen only nine wide receivers with a max-season mark that low. The only player even remotely relevant in fantasy was Russell Gage. That’s tough company. Where Bell gets interesting is in the versatility bucket. Like several others in this class, he was used as a Swiss Army knife in college. Unlike many of them, he brings legitimate size to the table. At Georgia, Bell logged 51 carries for 373 yards and 5 touchdowns. His usage lines up with that profile — he had one of the lower average depths of target in the class, and 18.4% of his collegiate receiving yards came on screens, sixth-highest among Combine-invited wide receivers. You’ll probably hear Deebo Samuel comparisons tossed around with Bell. Archetype-wise, it’s understandable. But Samuel entered the league with far stronger receiving production, and he had premium draft capital. Bell would need both opportunity and development to align perfectly to approach that kind of NFL impact. Bell’s not devoid of intrigue. Big, versatile players with backfield utility always catch attention. But analytically, this is a long runway bet.

Elijah Sarratt.lateround_profile: It’s no surprise we’re seeing Indiana wide receivers in this draft class given what that program has built over the last couple of seasons. Elijah Sarratt followed Curt Cignetti from James Madison to Indiana, finishing his career there, but his climb started well before that. A zero-star recruit, Sarratt began at Saint Francis before breaking out with an 82-catch sophomore season at James Madison. He then transferred to Indiana and posted 53 receptions for 957 yards and 8 touchdowns in 2024. That season marked Sarratt’s best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt, but the bigger takeaway is consistency. Across his three non–Saint Francis seasons, he posted marks between 2.58 and 2.75. He’s one of only three wide receivers in this class with multiple adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt seasons above 2.5 — and the only one to do it three times. The ZAP Model doesn’t fully reward that steadiness, as Sarratt lands with a 51.0 Breakout Score (71st percentile), but the broader profile is strong. Sarratt posted a 2.56 career yards per route run (top-six in the class) and gained a first down on 12.5% of his routes, trailing only Makai Lemon. There is some nuance, though. Reception Perception’s Matt Harmon has flagged separation concerns, and the data offers mild support: over 28% of Sarratt’s career targets were contested, per PFF. That’s not disqualifying — especially for a bigger-bodied receiver — but it does introduce volatility. Among Day 2 wideouts since 2020 with contested target rates above 25%, the range of outcomes includes Tee Higgins and Nico Collins on the high end, and players like Denzel Mims, Terrace Marshall, and Keon Coleman on the lower one. Alec Pierce and Michael Wilson have settled somewhere in between, but keep in mind that it took time for them to get there. The lack of separation, however, could be why we’re seeing Michael Thomas and Pat Bryant as top comps, two players who moved to more slot work in the NFL. The ZAP Model views Sarratt favorably overall. Analytically, he may be the most intriguing bigger-bodied receiver outside of Round 1 in this class. That doesn’t eliminate the variance. It just frames it.

The production at SMU was… fine. Nothing more. Hudson’s receiving yards per team pass attempt cleared 1.0 just once, and that came in 2025, his final year. Donaven McCulley.lateround_profile: Donaven McCulley’s path to wide receiver is one of the more unique ones in this class. McCulley arrived at Indiana in 2021 as a quarterback. That experiment didn’t last long — he completed just 35 of 82 pass attempts as a freshman before making the switch to wide receiver. Per a USA Today piece, McCulley noted that he’d grown taller and always had good hands, so he made the switch. The athleticism was obvious. The position just needed to change. Year 2, his first as a receiver, was understandably quiet. But in Year 3, we saw real progress: 48 receptions for 644 yards and a 1.91 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. For a converted quarterback, that’s respectable. After appearing in just four games for Indiana in 2024, McCulley transferred to Michigan to close out his career. He slightly edged his previous efficiency mark, improving his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt by 0.02. According to Rich Hribar of Sharp Football Analysis, no wide receiver in this draft class had a higher uncatchable target rate than McCulley. That likely suppressed his counting stats and may have capped what could’ve been a slightly better Breakout Score (he finished at 34.8). At 6’5” with legitimate athletic traits, there’s clearly clay to mold here. The issue is that the underlying efficiency never truly popped. His career yards per route run sits at 1.56. And that wasn’t meaningfully better on the perimeter, where he’s almost certainly destined to line up in the NFL. It’s a fun story and an intriguing athletic bet. A team could absolutely take a late-round swing on the size and developmental upside. But from a fantasy perspective, this is still a long-range projection rather than a profile built on proven production.

At its core, it’s simple: car soccer. You control a car and try to smash a ball into your opponent’s goal. I’ve played it on and off for years. It’s one of those games you can jump into for 20 or 30 minutes, knock out a few five-minute matches, and move on. When you queue up for an online game, Rocket League pairs you with teammates and opponents who have similar skill ratings. In theory, everyone on the field should be roughly equal. In practice? Not always. You can play with friends, but if you queue solo for a two-on-two or three-on-three match, you’re paired with random teammates. And those players can be total wildcards. Within the first minute or two, you usually figure it out. Maybe your teammate is clearly outmatched. Maybe they’re excellent, but their rating is suppressed by bad luck or poor teammates in previous games. Your expectations adjust quickly once you see real information. The same thing happens in fantasy football. Prospects enter the league with a set of expectations based on what we knew about them coming out of college. Then they play actual NFL games. Suddenly, we have new data that either reinforces or challenges those original assumptions. The question is: how much should those expectations change? That’s where the Year 2 Model comes in. The goal of the Year 2 Model is to do a better job predicting a player’s future performance — specifically his Year 2 through Year 4 B2S — than simply leaning on his rookie-season points per game. At both running back and wide receiver, the Year 2 Model combines a player’s prospect score, certain rookie-season production inputs, and his rookie-year fantasy points per game to generate a Year 2 score. That score is designed to recalibrate expectations using both pre-draft information and what we actually saw on the field. In previous iterations, the model focused on a player’s best season in either Year 2 or Year 3. This year, that window has been expanded through Year 4. Why? Because it gives us a larger, more stable sample to work with. Expanding the window allows the model to better capture delayed breakouts, injury interruptions, and uneven early-career development — all things that can distort shorter evaluations. As you’d expect, the Year 2 Model does a much better job predicting a player’s next three seasons than rookie-year points per game alone. Just like the ZAP Model, the Year 2 Model can be summarized through probability tables. Players are grouped into the same categories you’ve already seen — Legendary Performer, Elite Producer, and so on — allowing us to clearly see how often different tiers of players go on to hit specific B2S thresholds. The Year 2 Model is more predictive than the ZAP Model. Period. That’s not because the ZAP Model is flawed. It’s because we simply have more information. Once a player has completed his rookie season, we’re no longer projecting how he might handle NFL competition — we’ve seen it. We’re deeper into the Rocket League match now. We’ve watched our teammate play. The skill rating makes more sense. One thing you may notice when looking at the tables above is that the Year 2 Model behaves a little differently at running back than it does at wide receiver. Every elite Year 2–4 wide receiver lands in the Weekly Starter tier or above. At running back, though, a few productive players slip through the cracks. There are real football reasons for that. Running back production is often dictated by coaching decisions. If a staff wants a split backfield, it gets one. And for fantasy purposes, split backfields are a problem — they lower ceilings and introduce volatility. A running back can have a strong rookie season, but if his team decides to change roles, add competition, or rethink usage, that production doesn’t always carry forward. Wide receivers are different. There are more of them on the field. More routes. More chances to earn targets. When opportunity expands, talent tends to win out. That’s ultimately what these models are trying to capture — talent — and it’s why wide receiver outcomes are generally more predictable. Running back is also a more replaceable position. That doesn’t mean backs aren’t valuable; it means teams know they can find usable production later in the draft or in free agency. When supply is high, demand drops — and that creates instability. That instability shows up clearly in the data. From 2011 through 2023, there were six running backs selected after Pick 100 who averaged at least 12 PPR points per game as rookies (minimum seven games played, relevant fantasy weeks only). When looking at those players’ performances from Year 2 through Year 4, every single one of them peaked as a rookie. None improved their fantasy production after Year 1. Ask yourself why that might be. From a general manager’s perspective, a Day 3 rookie having a big season doesn’t automatically mean he’s a long-term solution. Was it real talent? Or was it circumstance? Scheme? Injuries elsewhere? Front offices will likely second guess themselves, wondering why that running back fell to the third day of the draft in the first place, regardless of his rookie-season production. Because running backs are relatively easy to replace — and relatively inexpensive — teams often introduce competition quickly. That competition suppresses long-term fantasy ceilings, especially for later-round players. In other words, rookie-year running back production is incredibly volatile. That volatility is exactly why the Year 2 Model matters. It properly weighs rookie production within the broader context of prospect quality, role stability, and historical outcomes. It’s not taking Year 1 numbers at face value. Journey Comparables At some point, every fantasy analyst ends up asking the same question: Have we seen this before? When something weird happens in the league, we ask it. When a player posts an absurd stat line, we ask it. When a team wins nine straight games by a field goal or less, we ask it. The Year 2 Model helps answer that question through Journey Comparables. You’ve already seen statistical comparables tied to the ZAP Model. Journey Comparables are different. Instead of comparing player attributes as they enter the league, they compare players based on how their evaluation changes. Specifically, Journey Comparables look at a player’s ZAP score alongside his Year 2 Model score and identify historical players who followed a similar path — from pre-draft expectations to post- rookie-season reality. They ignore play style, size, athletic testing, and role. The only thing that matters is the journey: how a player was viewed entering the league, and how that view changed after Year 1. History doesn’t repeat itself perfectly. For a lighter back, you’d ideally want one of those areas to pop. The odds of Claiborne developing into a true bell-cow are slim, particularly since he never fully owned that type of role in college. The more realistic path is a complementary or committee role in an NFL backfield. And for this archetype, opportunity tends to require both patience and the right ecosystem — if it comes at all.

Isaiah Bond.lateround_profile: Out of the 200-plus undrafted wide receivers in the ZAP Model, Isaiah Bond’s 23.1 score ranks sixth-best. Undrafted wideouts rarely go on to succeed at the NFL level, but Bond’s situation was a little different. He slipped out of the draft due to off-the-field issues and eventually signed with Cleveland in mid-August. And all things considered, his rookie season wasn’t, like, horrific. No one truly got it done for the Browns last year outside of Harold Fannin, but Bond finished second on the team in wide receiver targets. He trailed Jerry Jeudy in yards per route run by just 0.04, and while Cleveland’s offense struggled to sustain drives, Bond still accounted for roughly 9.5% of the team’s first-down receptions when active — a top-12 first-down share among last year’s rookie class. Still, peripherals like these rarely translate into long-term fantasy production. Bond finished just below the 1.0 yards per route run mark, and in the Year 2 Model database, only DJ Chark and Davante Adams have gone on to post WR2 seasons or better in any of the following three years after failing to reach that threshold as rookies. And even if Bond beats those odds — do we really feel great about Cleveland’s offensive environment in the short term?

Josh Cameron.lateround_profile: Josh Cameron often looks like a running back playing wide receiver. He’s got a thick, sturdy build, and Baylor leaned into that across his five seasons there. He wasn’t used out of the backfield much, but he also wasn’t asked to win deep — his career deep-ball target rate was the second-lowest in this class. His average depth of target sat a bit higher because he lived in that intermediate range. Cameron is more of a traits bet than a production one. He’ll be past his 23rd birthday at draft time, and he had five seasons to post a truly strong receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. It never happened. His best adjusted season came in at 1.90, and that translated to a 28.1 Breakout Score — just 30th percentile in the model. The ZAP Model isn’t overly enthusiastic. But one input does quietly like him: adjusted fantasy points per game. Among the 46 Combine invite wideouts, Cameron ranks 15th in that metric after age and schedule adjustments. That at least tells us he wasn’t totally unproductive. There are some interesting attributes here — size, play strength, and a profile that suggests he can handle physical work over the middle. Cameron just never showed high-end dominance. Cameron is the type of player who could hang around as a depth receiver and surprise if things break right. But unless the draft really swings in his favor, he’s not someone you need to aggressively prioritize in rookie drafts.

Harrison Wallace.lateround_profile: It’s tough to find an analytical hook in Harrison Wallace’s profile that screams upside. His Breakout Score sits below 30, he’ll already be 23 by draft day, his career yards per route run is just 1.86, and his best adjusted fantasy points per game season topped out at 12.7. He gained a first down on only 8.9% of his routes — an ordinary rate — and he doesn’t bring overwhelming size to compensate. Across the board, it’s hard to build a strong fantasy case. Wallace spent four years at Penn State before transferring to Ole Miss for his final season. He didn’t clear 300 receiving yards in any of his first three seasons with the Nittany Lions, and while his final two years produced adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt marks of 2.00 and 1.96, those are more “fine” than impactful. Just 14% of his routes came out of the slot, so he was mostly deployed as a perimeter guy. But there’s just not much separation in the data — literally or figuratively. Wallace might hear his name called late in the draft, but even if he does, the profile suggests a depth receiver fighting for a roster spot rather than someone likely to matter in fantasy formats.

Barion Brown.lateround_profile: Barion Brown is fast. Legit fast. A former track athlete, he was heavily involved as a kick and punt returner across four collegiate seasons — three at Kentucky and one at LSU. He showed off that speed at the Combine, running a 4.40 40. And, all things considered, the production profile isn’t terrible. At his size and with his speed, he likely projects as more of a gadget or space-oriented receiver at the next level. Historically, those players aren’t consistent fantasy league- winners. But there’s at least something to work with. Brown posted a 1.97 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark as a freshman — the best season of his career — which helped generate a 47.6 Breakout Score (66th percentile). That’s a strong number. Brown’s usage looks exactly how you’d expect for a player with his profile. He ranked top-10 among Combine-invited wide receivers in both behind-the-line and deep-ball target rates. Roughly 18% of his collegiate receiving yards came on screens — comfortably a top- 10 figure in this class. In other words, most of his opportunities came on manufactured touches or vertical shots. That doesn’t scream nuanced route technician — it suggests a player offenses were trying to scheme into space or send downfield to generate explosives. Again…speed. There’s intrigue because traits like his are rare enough to warrant attention. But this archetype tends to require overwhelming athletic dominance to truly hit in fantasy. Brown has juice. The question is whether it’s enough to overcome the limitations in his route profile and overall usage history.

Brenen Thompson.lateround_profile: Brenen Thompson spent one year at Texas, two at Oklahoma, and one at Mississippi State, where he finished his college career. He didn’t eclipse 250 receiving yards in a season until that final campaign, when he broke out for 57 receptions, 1,054 yards, and 6 touchdowns. That’s the textbook definition of a late breakout. And it shows in a 35.4 Breakout Score, a 41st percentile mark. Size is the bigger concern for fantasy. At 5’9”, 164 pounds, Thompson falls into a bucket that historically hasn’t produced many true difference-makers. In the ZAP Model database, the only sub-180-pound wide receivers to post a 15-plus PPR points-per-game season within their first three years are first-rounders DeVonta Smith and Jordan Addison. Smaller receivers can still offer usable production — think Josh Downs or Darnell Mooney — but many of those mini-hits came with significantly stronger Breakout Scores than Thompson’s. That said, his size shouldn’t automatically remove him from consideration. Interestingly, he barely played in the slot in college, running just 12.2% of his routes there, per PFF. On only 86 slot routes, though, he posted a ridiculous 4.15 yards per route run, the highest mark in the class. It’s a small sample, but it suggests some untapped deployment flexibility. And then there’s the 4.26 speed. His ability to stress defenses vertically — Thompson posted the fifth-highest deep-ball target rate in the class — could boost his draft capital. And if that happens, his ZAP score will rise with it. NFL teams covet that type of field-stretching juice, and stronger investment increases the odds he earns early snaps. Just don’t let the athleticism cloud the bigger picture. As we know, teams often overdraft this kind of speed. Thompson is intriguing — his comps are actually pretty fun, all things considered — but the overall profile still calls for tempered expectations.
